text: *Continuation of the Yijian Zhi* Song Dynasty Yuan Hao Wen

**Continuation of the Yiji Zhi** **Yishan** was a person from the Central Plains. If he had lived during the Xining and Fengzhen periods of the Song Dynasty, he would have been famous alongside Su Shi, Huang Tingjian, and others. Unfortunately, he was born during the reign of the Jin Dynasty. Although he lived in a border region and used literature to support military affairs, and though he was selected through the imperial examinations, he was unfortunate enough to live during the reign of Emperor Xuanzong of Jin, and thus did not hold a position of power or influence in the literary or administrative circles. Therefore, he wrote while living in seclusion. His writings and poetry are expansive, unrestrained, and free-flowing. The collection of works by his compatriots, known as *Zhongzhou Ji* (Central Plains Collection), contains detailed accounts of their careers, official positions, and seclusion, with no single line of poetry omitted. The fate of Song Dynasty scholars who fell into the hands of the Jin Dynasty is generally recounted at the end of the collection. His writing follows historical methods, and his heart that loves righteousness and goodness is truly vast! The continuation of *Yiji Zhi* that he wrote surpasses even Hong Jinglu's work! This can be seen from his own preface. He praised good deeds and punished bad ones, recording even the smallest details, so that one can understand the customs and see the hearts of people. Is there any difference between the North and South? Northern books were mostly carved during the Jin Dynasty and rarely reached the South. My friend Wang Qishan saw one and quickly copied it into a volume. His rich learning and diligent writing are evident. He brought it to show me as the day was ending, and I read until the third watch of the night, finishing four volumes. It greatly enlightened my heart! Because of illness, I could not copy it, but I noted it at the end of the volume and returned it. On the first day of the year in the Ren Shen year, Shangqiu's Song Wuzixu wrote this at the place where white egrets rest. **Once I have traversed the Central Plains, where shall I search for a solitary grave?** My achievements were never mine, but your writings are truly counted among the great! Your literary traditions stem from *Shiji* and *Hanshu*, and your insightful remarks surpass those of Jī Yun. I, too, am one who values the extraordinary, and how did I expect to gain such broad knowledge? Wu Dao Fu Jingwen.

Zǐsīzi said: "When a state is about to rise, there will be auspicious signs; when a state is about to fall, there will be ominous signs." Hóng Jǐnglú's *Economic and Political Miscellany* contains many stories from the reigns of Emperor Huizong and Emperor Ningzong; Yuán Hǎowèn's *Continued Economic and Political Miscellany* contains many stories from the reigns of Emperor Xuanzong and Emperor Xianzong. In a peaceful era, such matters are just a matter of leisure! ——砦窳叟 Wáng Qǐshàn of Wú, diligent and studious, whenever he encountered rare books, he would copy them by hand, which is one of his virtues. According to *Continued Economic and Political Miscellany*, it was compiled by Yuán Hǎowèn during the decline of the Central Plains, and all the stories were based on firsthand accounts, unlike Hóng Jǐnglú's *Economic and Political Miscellany*, which often included fictional elements. Its encouragement of good deeds and warning against evil are not without benefit. I know that Wáng Qǐshàn's intention to spread knowledge is the same as Yuán Hǎowèn's. ——Zhu Fāngshíyán Mínzhān, third year of Zhisun Yuán Hǎowèn's *Continued Economic and Political Miscellany* consists of two volumes, which are copies by Wáng Qǐshàn of Wú, and are now owned by Jièfǔ Xiàhóu. On the seventeenth day of the third month, leap year, of the twenty-third year of Zhizheng (1363 AD), I borrowed the manuscript and transcribed it, completing the task on the seventh day of the fourth month, at the Snow Reflection Study in the village of Sibei and in Huating. ——Sūn Dàomíng, aged sixty-seven, a lay follower at home I transcribed four volumes of *Continued Economic and Political Miscellany* from a jujube-bound manuscript from the north, which was indeed written by Yuán Hǎowèn. In the year of the Earth Rabbit (1360 AD), a new edition of the *History of the Jin Dynasty* was published in Wulin, and I had the opportunity to read it. I carefully recorded this preface and appended it to the manuscript. ——Wáng Dōngqǐshàn of Wú, on the Festival of Flowers, third year of Zhisun. (The preface itself is not recorded.)

Preface by Yu Ji Mr. Yuwen Xuzhong, during the late period of the Jin Dynasty, held a low-ranking position, fluctuating between various minor offices. He was repeatedly appointed to the Left Censorate but soon suffered from chaos and disaster, leading to his eventual downfall. In his later years, he took it upon himself to engage in literary pursuits, with a particular interest in national history, though he was often discouraged by others. Beyond poetry and prose, he expressed his thoughts in the *Zhongzhou* anthology. It is indeed true that scholars often face adversity rather than success. This *Yiji Zhi*, although a continuation of Hong Mai's work, was compiled during a time of great change, marked by frequent disasters and the imbalance of the five elements. It signifies the beginning of a new era and is closely tied to the workings of fate. It is not merely a collection of strange and ominous words but also a vehicle for auspicious and ominous signs. Not being a great man, I recognize small things, so why should I be criticized? When I came to Henan, the distinguished magistrate Rong Qing presented me with a copy from the *Read Yi* Tower, the collection of his father, Mr. Yuwen Junpu. The book consists of two volumes, but the prefaces by Song Zixu and Wang Qishan both state that it was originally four volumes, and they mention that it had a separate preface, which is missing from the current version. It is no longer possible to verify this. The book mostly contains trivial matters from the Eastern Capital. When I first arrived, I greatly benefited from it. I corrected its errors and restored its original structure, dividing it into four volumes. I hand-copied and printed it, and following Wang Zhen's compilation of Yuwen Xuzhong's *Chronological Biography*, I briefly outlined his life in an appendix to provide readers with a rough understanding of his life. This was written on a winter day in the year of Wu Chen during the reign of Emperor Jiaqing, by Yu Ji from Hangzhou at the Great Liang Academy. The appendix is not included.

**Treasure of the Treasury** Zhao Wang Rong successfully refined an elixir of immortality but did not consume it. It was stored in the treasury of Zhenzhou for over three hundred years. In the early years of the Zhenyuan era, the military commander of Zhending, Sanxi, abandoned the city and took the pill with him. When he reached Pingyang, he was impeached by Xushen Gong, and the pill was seized. The pill was brought to Kaifeng and stored in the Fengyan Treasury, where it was named "Treasure of the Treasury." After the chaos in the capital, I went with Liu Yanei, the Director of the Ministry of Revenue, to see it. The pill was stored in a lacquer cabinet, with a painting of Guangchengzi asking about the Dao on the side. Inside, there was another lacquer box, five inches high and three inches wide, with nine rings on the lid—eight on the outside and one in the middle, all coated with gold, each with a channel for the sound to flow through, and small holes in the rings. I thought it was the result of nine transformations. Inside the lacquer box, there was another silver box containing the pill. On the lid of the silver box, there was an engraved Buddha, with a dragon on the left and a phoenix on the right, at the feet of the Buddha, all also coated with gold. When opened, the pill was unusually light, with cracks running in all directions, the color like the bark of a jujube tree, and completely different from the cinnabar of today. I thought little of it. I asked the person in charge of the treasury what was special about it, and they said, "Nothing special, just that it constantly emitted strange light in dim conditions, like a fire." I personally saw it in the year Renchen.

The Golden Lion** In the early years of the Zhengda era, Zhang Shengyu was a guest in the northern street of Wuyang County. One day, a maid from his household bought a cow's kidney from a bowman's family. When she tried to cut it with a knife, the blade could not penetrate. Upon peeling it open, they found a stone shaped like a lion, colored as if coated with gold leaf. The stone had one paw bent inward and another resting under its head, as if it were sleeping. It emitted light every night. The stone was about two inches high and was likely formed by unusual forces. Zhang Shengyu's sister-in-law, Wu, collected the stone. It is unknown whether it still exists today. Zhang Shengyu recounts this. **The Dreams of Kang and Li** In the twelfth month of the year Renchen, Kang Bolu and Li Qins叔 traveled to Hezhong. The day before the city was captured, Kang and Li sought dreams from a certain deity. Kang Bolu dreamed that the city god broke the city, and he struggled to board a boat but fell into the water. A beautiful woman in fine clothing pulled him out. As he was pulled out, he saw a field of peach blossoms. Li Qins叔 dreamed that someone gave him two peach wood charms, inscribed with the words: "May you enter the new year with longevity and wealth." The next day, the city was captured. Kang Bolu failed to board the boat and drowned; Li Qins叔 managed to get on the boat and escaped to Shan County. After a few days, the new year began. The governor of Shan County, Yang Zhengqing, sent peach wood charms with the same inscriptions as in Li Qins叔's dream. Yang Zhengqing recounts this.

The Girl of the Bao Family Gets Married According to popular belief, Bao Xiwen served as the righteous and swift retribution officer of Mount Dong. This is known to all common people in the mountains and countryside. In the autumn of the Gengzi year, a woman from the south of Taian was captured by soldiers during a campaign and returned. She claimed to be the granddaughter of Bao Xiwen and had considerable beauty. A courtesan wanted to buy her at a high price, but the woman refused to go, even to the point of death. The master of the household coveted her wealth and subjected her to severe beatings, causing her to fall ill. The neighbors pitied her but could not help. A village woman who practiced witchcraft privately told someone, "I can free this woman and marry her to a good man." She then went to the master's house, closed her eyes and exhaled deeply, bending and stretching for a long time, acting as if a god had descended. After a while, she closed her eyes and shouted angrily, calling the owner out and cursing him severely. The owner prepared incense and fire, prostrated himself, and asked for forgiveness, wondering what he had offended the great god. The witch cursed again, saying, "I am the swift retribution officer! How dare you make my granddaughter a courtesan? I give you ten days. If you do not marry her to a good family, I will destroy your family!" The master bowed a hundred times to apologize, and within a few days, she was married.

Insect in Iron The abbot of our temple, residing at the mountain cliff of Feixu, said: "When I first entered the temple, the monk in charge of the kitchen reported: 'There is a large cooking pot in the kitchen that can serve a thousand people, but it makes a sound when heated. It has been this way for two years. People believe that the sound of the cauldron is inauspicious, so they abandoned it and did not dare to use it, hindering the making of food for the community. What should the teacher do about it?' The abbot replied: 'I will ask the community to give me this pot, and I will take care of it.' The community agreed. So he pounded the bottom of the cauldron and found a creature in the hole, about two inches long, deep red in color. This creature makes a sound when heated. Mr. Yang Shuneng of Zichuan also once saw a large hole in the cauldron at Junqing Temple on Mang Mountain, about the size of a clenched fist, containing a red grub-like creature. Such creatures are often seen by many. Emperor Wei Wen's *Dian Lun* states that fire is intensely hot and should not produce living things. It is merely a strict doctrine! The matter of the mountain cliff is entirely based on the theory of consciousness-only."

**Wang Zengshou, the External Force** In the southeast of Xiurong, in Shuangbao, there lived Wang Zengshou, known as "Wai Li" (the External Force), who was skilled in *jiao di* (wrestling or grappling), and no one could match his strength. At the end of the Taihe era, the officials seized camels. They nailed the camels’ feet to make them lame, and Wang Zengshou carried one of them from Yangtou Village all the way to Dazhou. The governor of the state believed this to be true, and Wang Zengshou carried the camel back again. Fang Shuai said: **Snakes and Scorpions in the Stone** During the Taihe era, Master Zhi Xian, an elder monk from Boshan, resided at the Western Jin Hall in Western Jin. He often kept a full set of footwear ready, but when he saw this, he immediately left. While repairing the road over Qiao Ling in Luyuan, he struck a large stone shaped like a bull’s heart with a mallet, breaking it open. Inside were snakes and scorpions biting and stinging each other. No one knew how they had entered. Master Zhi Xian said, “This is according to my method. These creatures are transformed from hatred and poison, entering according to one’s thoughts, having endured countless lifetimes without resolution. If not dispelled, one day they will say they have seen me come.” He struck it with a large staff, but nothing unusual happened. This is fully in line with the theory of consciousness-only. **The Elderly Couple of the Ren Family** In Shasha Village, Dingxiang, where Fang Shuai lived, it is said that Ren Shi and his wife Zhang were both seventy-three years old, born on the same day, month, year, and hour, and died on the same day, month, year, and hour. Such an event is without precedent in history. **Old Man Zheng and the Earth Taboo** Old Man Zheng violated the earth taboo.

In the southern village of Hantou, Pingyu, there lived an old man named Zheng Er, who had a strong character and did not believe in taboos. In the eighth year of the Taihe era, when his family was building something in the southeast of their home, someone said that the site was where the Tai Sui (a term in Chinese astrology) was located and should not be disturbed. Zheng replied, "I am the Tai Sui, what should I fear?" and supervised the laborers to begin construction. When they dug the ground to a depth of not two feet, they found a pair of red embroidered shoes belonging to a woman. The laborers wanted to stop the work, but Zheng became angry, took the shoes and burned them, and dug the ground more urgently. After digging another two or three feet, they found a black fish, which they cooked and ate. Within ten days, Zheng's mother also died, and he lost his eldest son. More than ten people were affected, including ten horses and forty cattle. Deaths and illnesses were scattered. The survivors were greatly afraid and fled to another place. The disaster then ceased. **Zhang's Son Enters the Underworld** In the southern village of Hantou, Pingyu, there lived an old man named Zhang, who made a living by catching quails, and people therefore called him Quail. He was old and had only one son, who had grown into a young man. One day, the son died. The old man and his wife thought of their own old age with no one to rely on, wept and fainted, hating that they could not die together. The next day, they wanted to bury him, but could not bear to do so. They simply piled up bricks to make a mound, digging the ground to a depth of one or two feet. They said, "Our son will come back to life!" Some people laughed at their foolishness, while others pitied them. Three days later, they returned to the tomb, weeping uncontrollably. Suddenly, they heard a moaning sound from inside the tomb. The old man and his wife were startled and said, "Our son has indeed returned to life!" They removed the bricks and dragged the coffin out, carrying him back home. The son indeed came back to life. Soon, he asked for soup and porridge. After a while, he said he was first taken by someone to the underworld. The son pleaded with the officials in tears: "My parents are old and pitiable. I beg to live out my remaining years, to finish the funeral rites, and then die without regret." The officials of the underworld pitied him and said, "Now we will let you return."

The father was told, "If you can abandon your trade of hunting and fishing, your son's life can be prolonged!" Upon hearing this, the father burned all his nets and traps, took his son to the temple to worship Buddha. There was a monk named Lu in the temple, who was not yet forty years old, with a distinguished appearance, and had once served as the head of a convoy in the state. Zhang the boy then asked the monk, "Did you also come back from the dead?" The monk replied, "How could I have died?" Zhang said, "I saw you in the underworld, tied with an iron rope around your feet on a copper pillar in the corner of a hall, and guards were hitting you with sticks on your armpit, causing you to bleed profusely." When he was released, he asked the guard why Master Lu was punished. The guard said, "He often took down the scriptures of the lay devotee, so he received this retribution." Master Lu was greatly startled. It turned out that he had a leaking sore on his armpit for three years! The son did not know about it at first. Master Lu then lived in a clean room, reciting scriptures as his daily practice. After three years, the sore healed. Zhao the official saw it with his own eyes. Earthly Taboo 2 In the spring of the year Yishisi (乙巳), a student from Huizhou named Huamen led his servant to dig the ground and found a piece of meat, about the size of three or four sheng (升). They cut it with a knife, and the meat was like that of a sheep, with skin and membranes. The servant said, "A piece of meat in the earth is said to be the Tai Sui (太岁), and seeing it brings bad luck; it should not be dug up." The student said, "How could I know there is a Tai Sui?" He ordered them to dig again, and they found two more pieces of meat. Within half a year, deaths followed one another, and all the cattle and horses died. The ancients said, "To deliberately violate an ominous sign, is to defy the gods!" Shen Huru, a neighbor, witnessed it and told me about it.

**Crowds of Bears** At the beginning of the year Jiwei, tens of thousands of bears emerged from the Xiaoshi of Neixiang and entered the southwestern mountains. They advanced silently and in unison. After traveling a great distance, their palms bled. Those who were weak or inferior and died were eaten by the other bears. The prefectures and counties had official documents transmitted and reported. I witnessed this in Changde. **The Sword Blooming with Flowers** In the year Xin Chou, a great sword at the Guan Yu temple in Jiyuan suddenly grew ten or so stems of flowers, each about an inch long, as fine as hair. The stems were slightly green, and the tips bore delicate white flowers larger than millet grains. My fellow resident, Li Qing's son Zhengfu, told me about this. **Birth of a Dragon** In the summer of the year Yi Si, in the village of Weipo in Pingding, a woman named Ma Shipo, around fifty years old, had been pregnant for more than six years. This year, she finally gave birth to a dragon. The officials asked about the circumstances. The woman said that she had been pregnant for three to four years without giving birth, and her husband, Cao Zhubu, fearing it would be a sign of an omen, sent her away. As she was about to give birth, she suddenly saw people lined up in front of her, as if she were in an official court. One person stepped forward and said, "I have been entrusted to you for several years, and now it is time for me to leave. Next year, Mother will be happy!" After speaking, a white-clad person supported her and took her away. She reached the gate and fainted, losing consciousness. After a long time, she regained consciousness. Others told her that there were three thunderclaps in the darkness, and the dragon flew away from her body, leaving the place of her pregnancy unknown.

Unusual Governance by Xian Bian During the reign of Emperor Zhenyou, Xian Bian served as the governor of Shouzhou. One day, while Yang Jin was returning from a patrol, suddenly a yellow dog appeared in front of his horse, wagging its tail gently and playfully, walking and looking back as if guiding someone. Xian Bian sent two soldiers to follow the dog, which led them directly to a dry well on the west riverbank. The soldiers lowered their heads and looked down into the well. Approaching to observe, they found a trace of blood at the edge of the well and a body inside. They immediately rushed back to report to Xian Bian and called the landowner to guard the well. The dog then led them back into the city, where they saw a guesthouse. The dog barked continuously, as if it had something to report. Xian Bian called the owner of the guesthouse. The owner recognized the dog and said, "This dog belongs to Mr. Zhu. A few days ago, he rented a boat on the west river and took this dog with him. Now the dog has come alone—why is that?" Xian Bian immediately arrested the boat owner and went with him to the county, ordering the owner to identify the body. The owner identified the body as belonging to the boat owner. Xian Bian then asked where Mr. Zhu was, and without further torture or interrogation, the boat owner immediately confessed. There was also a person named Zhou Li who was collecting firewood on New Temple Beach west of the state. He was eaten by a tiger. Zhou Li's wife cried and reported this to Xian Bian. Xian Bian said, "I will go once." He led more than ten servants and rushed to New Temple Beach among the dense undergrowth. There, they saw a tiger, which had flattened its ears and closed its eyes, slowly walking forward as if being driven and held by ghosts and gods. Xian Bian killed the tiger with an arrow. He cut open the tiger's belly and found a ring still in its body. This account was provided by Fan Sinao, who said it was true.

**Hearth Soil and Flesh** **He Xinsu**, from **Xuzhou**, passed the imperial examinations during the **Cheng'an** era. In the early **Chongqing** era, he returned to his hometown due to his father's passing. One night, he saw a strange light in the courtyard and said, "This must be a treasure!" He led his servants to dig it up. They dug about ten feet deep and found a block of flesh as large as a basin. The family was greatly alarmed and quickly buried it. Soon after, **He Xinsu** died of illness. His wife and over ten family members also died in succession. Scholars say, "The flesh was the **Tai Sui**! The disaster was about to strike, hence the strange phenomena appeared first."

The Disaster of Luxurious Living A man from Yan named Liu Boyu was wealthy and influential during the Dading period. By nature, he was extravagant and luxurious, refusing to eat unless it was a delicacy. His household housed hundreds of people, and he summoned all the cooks to reside there, often providing them with charity. He would ask about the quality of the meat and sometimes imitate their practices. When he grew old and fell ill, his wealth dwindled, and he died in sorrow and depression. Ten years after his death, his two sons went begging in the market. The disaster of luxurious living is a lesson that I have seen with my own eyes, and I cannot say how many others have suffered the same fate! I merely record this here. These two stories were also told by the Director of Agriculture. The Tomb of Jingniang The father of Wang Zongyuanlao, who served as the Director of Transport, was named Chu and served as the magistrate of Pingshan. When Wang was in his twenties, he first took the imperial examinations and studied in the garden behind the county office. One evening, while walking among the flower stones, he met a woman. He asked her name, and she replied, "I am the daughter of the previous magistrate Yang." Wang was pleased by her youthful beauty and gently teased her. The woman did not get angry but smiled, and so they had a relationship. On a later Cold Food Festival, Wang was invited by a friend to play with a ball in the empty land west of the garden. A servant pointed out the grave of Jingniang, and Wang asked who Jingniang was. His companions said, "She was the young daughter of the previous magistrate Yang, named Jingniang, who died at the age of 15 and was buried here." Upon hearing that Jingniang was the daughter of the previous magistrate Yang, Wang began to suspect her identity. Returning to his study, he waited a short while before the woman arrived. She wept softly, hesitating as she approached. She said to Wang, "You already know me, so what more can be said? The paths of the living and the dead are different, and it is also difficult to stay for long. The exam period is near, and you will surely pass. There will be minor difficulties in the middle, and even if you are sick, you should try your best to go. You will meet me on the road to Liaoyang."

After finishing his speech, he left. Soon after, the elder fell ill. His parents did not want him to undertake the journey. After a month, he slightly recovered, and the elder determinedly requested to go, having him carried in a cart. On the way, they reached the Liaohe Plain, where continuous heavy rain and muddy conditions made the cart unable to proceed. Those traveling with him whipped their horses to continue on the road, but the cart alone traveled several miles before its axle broke. The elder was distressed and did not know what to do. Suddenly, a peasant appeared, carrying an axe and a saw, and approached the cart. When asked, he identified himself as a craftsman. The elder sighed and said, "In this place, there are no human settlements for two hundred miles in either direction. Now encountering this craftsman, it is not by chance but by hidden assistance!" After repairing the axle, they were about to continue their journey when suddenly another cart appeared, and the person in the cart was none other than Jingniang. The elder was surprised and delighted, saying, "You have also come here?" Jingniang replied, "Do you not remember our conversation on the road to Liaoyang? Knowing you would face difficulties, I came to comfort you!" The elder asked, "Can you tell me where I will be going next?" Jingniang climbed onto the cart and simply said, "Minister, take care," before departing. Within a few days, the elder arrived at the capital and passed his examination. During the Mingchang period, he served as a transport commissioner. When the emperor participated in a ceremony at the Taishi, he acted as the Minister of Rites. A few days later, he died.

**Shenxiao Danbao** During the Xuanhe era, alchemists transformed mercury into gold and cast it into coins. Those in the Shenxiao palace had the inscriptions "Shenxiao Danbao"; those for the Five Blessings were inscribed with "Wufu Danbao"; and those for Taiyi were similarly inscribed. When Kaifeng fell, the coins went to the imperial treasury, and Emperor Wanyan Liang of the Jin Dynasty gave them to his favored officials. Those who received them used them as hat rings, believing that wearing them would prevent heatstroke. This was recounted by Wáng Shòu Sūn, the Director of the Imperial Treasury. **Slippery Painting** An elderly farmer from the Western Capital, who called himself "Clay Pot Child," was over seventy years old. He created a painting called "The People of Yao," using green silk as the base and birch bark for the painting. He made a small hole in a dark room to get light and painted while talking and laughing with his guests. Once, he playfully picked lice from his sleeves and scattered them onto his guests' clothes, who thought they were real lice and picked them up. His skill was remarkable. He was a stern and reclusive person, proud of his art, and did not easily share it with others. If something he did not want himself, he would not accept it even if offered a thousand gold pieces. Slippery painting is not recorded in books, and it is said to have started with this person. This is documented in the records of Zhang Boyiwen, the Director of the Transport Office in Pingyang.

枸杞** At the beginning of the Tahe era, the ancient city wall of Dingtao collapsed. A lycium root emerged, approximately one foot square, shaped like a lying dog. It had feet and a tail, and even fine hair around its mouth. A branch grew straight up from it. A farmer outside the county obtained it, and the village community passed it around to admire. Soon, it was taken by the county official. Cui Junzuo saw this when he was around fifteen or sixteen years old. **Poetic Prophecy** Liang Zhongjing, on his way to his official post, wrote a poem on the road to Xianping: "Mountain clouds signal rain, flowers look sad first; on the traveler's road, even birds are sad." Liu Yushi Yunqing wrote: "Broken walls, autumn lamps, dreams shattered; old wutong trees, cold rain, sorrow arises." Li Zhong Pingfu wrote: "Fallen leaves cannot be swept away; cold flowers, look at them and they are gone." Not long after, they all passed away. It is likely a poetic prophecy! As for Yang Minxing's poem "Daytime Sleep," which says: "Body like a cicada shedding its shell on a bed; dreams follow willow catkins for a thousand miles," it is truly ghostly language. What prophecy is there? **Poetic Prophecy of Cui Minzhi's Brother** On the Mid-Autumn Festival of the first year of the Zhenyou era, Cui Minzhi's brother planned to meet with Wang Yuanqing, Tian Deshou, and Tian Xianqing. But that night was dark and gloomy, and the gathering was canceled. Cui Minzhi wrote a poem: "On a good day, nothing comforts longing; first, I recited the poem from last night. Do not grow tired, wait until late night; dense clouds will occasionally clear." Wang and Tian joked, "The poetic realm is not open; has your talent run out?" Cui Minzhi sighed, "I will only live to be thirty; will the realm open up?" The next year, he suffered the disaster of the city's fall, and he was only thirty-two.

**Shen Bosheng's Poem as an Omen** Shen Wanquan of Gaoping, styled Bosheng, was a historian during the reign of Emperor Zhengda. He followed Prince Qingshan of the imperial clan on a southern campaign as a compiler of historical records. On the journey, he wrote a poem that said: "Turning back to the west wind, I bid farewell to my humble abode. Once again, I follow the military chariot through rugged terrain. The course of life is inherently uncertain, Like a single reed on the lake, I drift wherever fate takes me." A few days later, he drowned in the Huai River and died. **The Descent of Cranes at Tianqing Temple** In the western part of Xinzhou, half of the city lies on the Nine Dragon Ridge. There are the Temple of the Sage, the Iron Buddha Temple, and the Tianqing Temple, which serve as the city's landmarks. The Tianqing Temple has a grand statue of Laozi in its main hall, attended by statues of seven Tang emperors. The elders say that these statues were sculpted by divine beings. The temple was rebuilt in the second year of Tianfu during the Later Jin dynasty. Every year on the 15th day of the second lunar month, known as the "Zhengyuan Festival" in Taoism, cranes gather at the temple. They often come in dozens, but even a few are not uncommon. They soar and dance above the altar and halls, staying for a long time before departing. The people of the city gather to watch, and the city walls are crowded with spectators. The local governor decreed that those who see the cranes first would receive a reward. Taoist priests and tourists from afar came in an unbroken stream for three days. During the military chaos of the Zhenyou period, the temple was destroyed, and the cranes no longer came.

**Drought and the Drought Spirit** In the early years of the Zhenyou era, there was a severe drought in the Luoyang region. In Guo Cheng, forty miles west of Dengfeng, people said that a drought spirit was causing harm. The elders reported: "When the drought spirit appears, there is always a fire-like light accompanying it." They instructed young men to watch from a high place after dusk. Sure enough, they saw a fire-like light enter a farmer's house, and they struck it with a large club. The flame scattered, and there was a sound like galloping horses. According to ancient texts, the drought spirit is three feet tall and moves as swiftly as the wind. The description of a galloping sound is not commonly found in traditional accounts. **The Ghost of the Temple School** In Taiyuan, there was a temple school where a ghost woman appeared. She was the concubine of a high-ranking official during the Song dynasty. She was beaten to death by the official's wife and buried upside down beside the school, where a mulberry tree grew. This ghost sometimes entered the study rooms and played games with the students, but she did not cause any harm. During the Dading era, several students stayed overnight in the study room. After midnight, they suddenly heard footsteps outside the window. Soon, the ghost entered the room and touched each of the sleeping students, saying, "This one will pass the imperial examination, and this one will not." She then reassured them, saying, "Don't be startled, don't be startled." As predicted, all the students' outcomes matched her words. Scholar Ma Chizheng recounted this story. The students involved were Zhao Wenqing, Duan Guohua, and Guo Jizhi.

The Golden Horse of the Wang Family The Wang family from Taiyuan has been engaged in medicine for generations, accumulating hidden virtue known in the village. By the time of Junyu's father, both the grandfather and grandmother revered the gods and Buddha. In a clean room where scriptures and images were placed, the lock and key were very strict, and the grandmother personally did the sweeping and cleaning. One evening, while burning incense and reciting scriptures, a small object suddenly jumped out from under the table, followed by a light. In a moment, it made a sound like a horse neighing. The grandmother stood up and wished, saying, "Ancient legends speak of a golden foal; now I truly see it! If you truly wish to bring good fortune, come into the old woman's lapel!" She immediately welcomed it with her lapel, and the object jumped up once. Looking at it, it was a golden horse! Junyu passed the imperial examination in the second year of Tianjuan, and Qi Yu and Ru Yu passed the imperial examinations in the first year of Huangtong in succession. The villagers honored them, calling them the "Three Gui Wang Family." The governor also named the street where they lived "Three Gui." The grandfather had four sons, three of whom passed the imperial examinations, and one son was appointed through imperial favor. Down to his grandson Zhongze, who became a famous Jinshi (imperial scholar), his writing, governance, debate, and calligraphy were greatly admired by his contemporaries. The golden horse was three inches square, the gold was the color of dates, the neck was slightly raised, the tail was raised like a cone of mugwort, and the thighs and legs were round and smooth. After the chaos of war, I have seen it. Junzhou Qingqing Fangyue composed the "Ode to the Golden Horse."

Wang Yunhe Wang Zhongli, courtesy name Tangchen, was from Kailan. He had an extensive reading and a strong memory, knowing no question. In his youth, he studied the *Yi Jing* and was famous in the examination halls. His family was wealthy, and guests filled his door daily, treated with utmost generosity and luxury. However, his self-provision was merely a cup of plain soup and noodles each day. Before reaching forty, he lost his wife. He did not remarry, nor did he take up the imperial examinations, living alone in a room like a monk. After three to four years, he emerged. People noticed that his speech was lofty and broad, his poetry and paintings unparalleled, as if something mystical was attached to him. When asked, he remained silent. In the early years of the Da'an era, he met Zhao Gong, the leisurely scholar, in Pingding and left him a poem saying, "Entrust to the idle, free-spirited scholar, in vain, following poetry and wine, falling into worldly entanglements. Yellow dust blocks the path back, not reaching the fairyland for five hundred years." He then said, "Five hundred scholars of the Tang dynasty were all demigods who descended. Tainted by the flavors of the world, some are lost and do not return, like you and I are also such." One day, he came to the capital and stayed at Zhao Gong's house. His Mid-Autumn Festival poem included the lines, "The imprint penetrates the shadow of mountains and rivers, illuminating the heart of heaven and earth. The world has day and night, but my heart has no past or present." Zhao Gong found these lines extraordinary. He then asked for a bowl of ink and water, and as he said, he gave it to him.

The next morning, he left without saying goodbye. On the wall, he left the characters "龟鹤" (tortoise and crane), each one zhang in width and length. The ink was present, but it is unknown what wrote it. After a while, the master returned from outside, and when asked about the reason for this, he did not answer. He wrote beside the characters: "Between heaven and earth, there is one ancient Confucian scholar. Awake, he does not remember the writing done in a drunken state. Others mistakenly compare it to immortal characters, only fearing that immortal characters are not as good." The master's poems, such as: "In a drunken sleeve dance, he finds the sky and earth too narrow; his wild poetic passion presses the sea and mountains flat. Suddenly startled by the wind and waves passing by his ear, he unconsciously brings his spirit and form to the other world. Because of you, he is grateful and follows your words, breaking through the mechanism, even if there is, it is also shocking." There are many such poems. When someone asks about matters outside the world, he also answers with a few words. He likes to write large characters, which are extremely dynamic and lively. He loves them very much. The man from Pingshan named Li Zhipin once met the master, discussing the people of previous generations, citing dozens of points from ancient scholars in front of them, as if everyone were challenging each other, then judging them based on his own opinion. He considered this to be among the best in eloquence and knowledge. On the verge of death, he previously set the date of his passing, and died as he had predicted. He was forty-nine years old. In his later years, he changed his name to Yunhe and was nicknamed Pseudo-Xu Daoist. His appearance resembled a worldly painting of Mr. Lü, with slightly raised shoulders and ears.

**董国华** 董文甫，字国华，潞人，承安中进士。他天性淳朴，对世事无欲无求，人们都敬重他，却不知他为何能如此。他的儿子安仁，也学习道学。父子二人隐居在宝丰，闭门读书。虽生活困顿，却依然平静自若。董文甫曾任金昌府判官、礼部员外郎。正大年间，因公事前往杞县。他预知自己的死期，便写信给家人和同僚，并为杞县的县令和佐官作诗，多达三十多首。写完信后，他安然坐化。 **卫文仲** 卫文仲，襄城人，承安中进士。他性格淡泊，喜欢读书学道，因此在仕途上没有多大进展。平日里，他喜欢吟唱苏轼的《赤壁词》。临终时，他沐浴更衣，召来家人交代后事，随后命人关上门。他端坐于床，诵读《赤壁词》，又吟唱了最后两句。唱罢，他安详地去世了。

### 一行墓石记 Dai Bo Ji, after the military chaos of the Zhenyou era, was appointed as the governor of Huzhou and later transferred to Jizhou. Both civilian and official residences were burned down. Dai Bo Ji then rebuilt the state residence. While digging into a yellow earth slope, he accidentally encountered an ancient tomb, which turned out to be the tomb of the Tang dynasty monk Yi Xing. There was a stone inscription that said, "Dai Bo Ji will destroy my tomb." ### 明月泉 The Moonlight Spring is located in Wutai Mountain. When people arrive at the spring, they cover their eyes with silk or cloth and look down at the spring water, sometimes seeing the moon reflected in it, hence the spring's name. After thousands of people, only one or two have seen this phenomenon. Grand Censor Yang Shuyu, a native of Wutai, told me, "I personally saw the Moonlight Spring, not just heard about it!" ### 石守道心化石 The tomb of Stone Shoudao is located in Fengfu. During the Taihe period, the tomb collapsed, and his grandchildren prepared a coffin for burial. The bones were no different from those of ordinary people, but his heart was like two hands joined together, already turned to stone. ### 毁宝鼎 During the Huantong period, the Internal Works Department was burning glazed tiles. They destroyed a large tripod, which did not melt for three days. The tripod was about to break, and there was a sound like thunder, heard thirty miles away. People say: success and failure are determined by fate, and fate meets with calamity. Even divine objects cannot protect themselves, not just this tripod! Xiyang said. ### 田鼠 During the Zhengda Renxu period, farmers in the northern hills of Neixiang reported that field mice were eating crops. The mice were as big as rabbits, and they came in groups of ten or a hundred, leaving the fields they passed through barren. Hunters shot and caught several of them, some weighing more than ten pounds, with fur color similar to that of otters. I have never heard of such large mice before.

A Celestial Demon Affliction At the end of the Tahe era, Lei Jingpang served as the defense judge of Shouzhou, and his brother Xiyán also took up his post there. There was an official prostitute named Xiangxiang who was afflicted by a celestial demon, causing her to be mentally confused and sometimes sleep for several days without waking. Xiyán told his colleagues, “There is a woman possessed by a celestial demon who was taken to the top of a pagoda. Whatever she desired, the demon could immediately fulfill. One day, she saw a curtained carriage pass by the base of the pagoda and told the demon, ‘The wife of a nobleman is inside the carriage; go and fetch her hairpin!’ The demon went and came back after a long time, having found nothing. The woman asked why, and the demon replied, ‘That woman is blessed, and a spirit protects her, so I could not approach.’ The woman then asked, ‘Is it because she is the wife of a nobleman that she has a spirit to protect her?’ The demon answered, ‘It is not because she is a noblewoman, but because she does not eat beef!’ The woman immediately made a vow, ‘If I can be freed from this affliction, I will not only abstain from eating beef for the rest of my life, but I will also vow to encourage others to do the same.’ Before she finished speaking, the demon cursed and left, and it never returned. The woman cried out for help, and her family lowered her down with a rope, and she was ultimately saved. Since Xiangxiang could abstain from eating beef and made a vow before the gods and Buddhas, the demon could no longer approach her.” The colleagues relayed these words to Xiangxiang, and she made the same vow. After more than ten days, she came dressed in fine attire, holding wine to express her gratitude, saying, “Thanks to the teachings of the learned man, I am now a normal person!”

**Shen Ge** A wife from the family of an official in the Hanlin Academy, Sun Guozhen, was defiled by a *shan xiao*. The *shan xiao* said, "If you lack provisions, I can bring them to you instantly." Once, she had a pile of silk filling her courtyard, all marked with the seal of the Zhen Ding treasury. Her family, burdened by the possession of official property, begged for it to be removed. Soon after, the silk vanished without a trace. Then there appeared a white horse, adorned with a golden saddle and precious bridle, of unknown origin, yet tethered to the manger. The family became even more frightened. They prayed for a long time, and then the horse suddenly disappeared. The children whispered among themselves, calling the *shan xiao* "Wu Lang," and asked, "If a family has no heir, can you bring them a child?" The next morning, a child appeared, with a face as beautiful as a painting, wrapped in silk swaddling and embroidered襁褓, lying on the bed. The old and young of the family prayed and did not wish to accept him, but in the end, they kept him. They named him "Shen Ge." At the age of six, he died of illness.

Wang, being drunk, abused his younger brother's widow and orphaned children. The grandmother, Zhang, had endured Wang's behavior for a long time and had no one to turn to for help, so she fell ill and could not get up. One night, she slept with her daughters. In the middle of the night, when the lamp dimmed, she heard rustling sounds. Soon after, the checkers on the board were scattered, and there were sounds of clattering. She repeatedly sighed. The grandmother cried out in despair. She said, "My fifth uncle, drunk and abusive, cannot be controlled by the law. If this is not stopped, the mother and children will be at the mercy of others!" A few days later, Wang, drunk, left Dingxiang at night and returned. On the way to Zhao Village, he encountered his brother in the middle of the road, scolded him severely, and then beat him with a horsewhip wildly. Wang, holding his head, fled and barely made it home. When they lit a fire to examine him, his clothes were torn to pieces, and his back was covered with bruises. The next day, he bowed a hundred times in front of the image of his grandfather as a sign of apology. Afterward, he also stopped drinking.

Wang Quanmei's Mother's Poetic Words The mother of Wang Quanmei from Dingxiang had served Buddhism since her childhood. When she grew old, she served incense and candles even more diligently. She did not know how to read or write before, but one day, she told her brother, saying, "Nephew, I casually came up with a line. See if you can make a verse out of it." She recited several dozen lines, but only "The celestial mechanism cuts off the dream of luxury" seems to have come from her wisdom. The mother passed away shortly after. Strange Things about Lice After the fall of Dezhou, all civilian homes and government temples were burned down. There were dozens of cannons under the inner city, with ropes hanging in the old camp. Someone wanted to untie these ropes and saw that each rope was completely covered with large lice from top to bottom, like wax poured over a candle. It is said that after the attack on Bianjing (Kaifeng) it was the same. In the extreme chaos and disorder, what cannot be found in the world?

The Wife of a Man from Danshou In the early years of the Jurchen Jin Dynasty, Huang Jiu from Yuxian, who was part of the band of rebels known as "Zhutian Guai" from Fuer Gu, caused unrest in Shanfu and captured hundreds of elderly and young people. Among them was a woman of great beauty, whom Huang Jiu wanted to take by force. The woman pretended to the bandits, saying, "My husband will arrive shortly. I would like to see him before I marry you." When she saw her husband, she told him about the act of kidnapping and then pointed at Huang Jiu and cursed him with foul language. The bandits could not bear their anger and cut her down. The place where she was cut did not bleed, but white grease flowed instead. This is explained by the theory of Huang Guan and Yu Ji. The Wife of Dai Shi, Liang Shi Dai Shi, whose origins are unknown, lived in the left village of Luoyang after the chaos, working as a hired laborer. In the eighth month of the year Gengchen, an interpreter was herding horses in a bean field. Dai drove him out. The interpreter became angry and beat him to death with a whip. His wife, Liang Shi, carried the body to the camp to complain. The interpreter was a servant of a noble family and relied upon by his master. They offered two cows and a piece of white gold to Liang Shi to redeem the crime. They also said to her, "Your husband's death is also fate. Your two sons are young, and with money, you can support yourselves. Even if this person is killed, what good is it to the deceased?" Liang Shi replied, "My husband died without a crime, how can we talk about profit? But if this servant pays for his death, my mother and I will be content even if we have to beg for food!" The crowd could not persuade her. They said to Liang Shi, "Would you rather kill this person yourself?" Liang Shi replied, "What is there to be afraid of!" She then took a knife and wanted to cut him down herself. The crowd, fearing that this woman would hate the interpreter, did not let her kill him immediately, so they killed him. Liang Shi gathered blood and drank it, then took her two sons away. According to Zhai Zhizhong of Luoyang.

Li Zhou's Eyes Were Sick Li Zhou of Liaocheng had two sons; one was blind, and the other was born without eyes. Last year, one of his eyes dried up again. He asked the Daoist from Shentiao for treatment. The Daoist asked, "Has he ever done anything wrong?" Li replied, "All my life, I sculpted statues of gods, and whenever I needed eyeballs, I would often take them from collapsed statues. Is this the reason?" In the spring of Dingyou year, the Daoist came to Yangping and told me about it. Li was stingy with small expenses and thus received this retribution! A Human with a Tail Wang Bo of Qinghe, who makes a living as a tailor, was thirty-seven years old. One day, he visited the Daoist from Liaocheng and said: In early spring of Dingyou year, he was drunk and slept in a peach garden. Suddenly, he dreamed of a god in golden armor holding a halberd, who kicked him to make him get up. Wang asked, "Who are you?" The god replied, "I am sending you your tail." Afterward, he felt pain and itching in his coccyx. After several days, a tail grew, about the size of a finger, like the tail bone of a sheep that has shed its wool. He wanted to remove it, but the pain went through his heart and marrow, and even burning it didn't help. He said he was ungrateful to his mother, which led to his starvation, and thus he received this retribution. Every time someone looked at it, the pain and itching lessened a bit, but otherwise it was unbearable. He asked the Daoist for treatment. The Daoist had no way to treat it, so he left. Now he lives in Xin Dian. The Daoist from Shentiao said.

Zhi Gong's Hidden Virtue At the beginning of the Ming Dynasty, in Dingzhou Tang County, there was a man named Wang Ba, who was physically imposing and dignified, with remarkable physical strength. He was deceived by the chancellor into planning a rebellion. As a result, he set up a deceitful plan and enlisted the names of fellow villagers. Before he could begin to recruit, his plot was discovered, and he was arrested. The governor Gao, upon learning of this, arrested several thousand people according to the list. Gao wanted to charge everyone with treason. Zhi Gong, who was the commandant at the time, advised against it, saying, "By enlisting people's names through deceit, the crime should be limited to Wang Ba. Those who were not involved in the plot, it would be even worse to have them in the list of those misled, and how much more so to accuse them of rebellion?" The governor was not pleased and ordered another official to investigate the matter. The official, following the governor's orders, fabricated charges against them. However, the governor, influenced by Zhi Gong's words, began to doubt the fairness of the proceedings. The next day, he summoned Zhi Gong and asked, "Why do you go to such lengths to save these people?" Zhi Gong replied, "To clear the names of the innocent, what is there to fear in death?" After a long pause, the governor said, "You are right. I will reconsider this." The next day, he followed Zhi Gong's advice, executing the main culprits and releasing the rest. He also burned the old case files. Zhi Gong's son, Shi Jue, passed the imperial examination as the first-place winner in the early Tianjuan era and later retired as Left Chancellor. Therefore, Zhi Gong's hidden virtue was widely recognized by all villagers, regardless of age.

**Ma San's Deceitful Report** In Enzhou, Liu Ma San became wealthy through cunning and deceit. He once used trickery to take over the land of his neighbor Yuan Chuntian. Yuan Chuntian complained to the authorities, but Ma San produced a contract as proof and ultimately took the land. Yuan Chuntian could not accept this, and he told the villagers every day, "He has slandered and deceived like this, and has already become a different kind of being!" Ma San also swore to himself, "If I indeed fraudulently took your land, then I will do as you say!" In the second year of Taihe, Ma San died of illness. Yuan Chuntian's dog gave birth to several puppies, among which was a small spotted dog with pure white belly fur and a red inscription on its belly reading, "I am Liu Ma San of Enzhou." Ma San had many enemies, and he eventually wanted to buy the dog. The dog was soon taken by the state governor. The entire prefecture knew about it. The descendants of the Ma family could not bear the shame and bought the dog back to keep it at home. **White Shen Guan** In Luojiāo, Fuzhou, during the Dading era, there was a fraudster named Bai Shen Guan who could create strange phenomena using unorthodox methods. He could make dragons appear out of nowhere, pull out golden hands from his sleeves, or see Buddha statues while sitting. His tricks were so dazzling that people within hundreds of miles flocked to him, and no one dared to oppose him. My maternal uncle, Wang Jun, was the county magistrate at the time and captured him. When asked how he could create these strange phenomena, Bai Shen Guan claimed it was done by gods. Later, when they dug up a jar of fox saliva, Bai Shen Guan confessed his crimes. He was executed with two hundred strokes of the stick and died. The county became peaceful as a result. Later, my uncle Yan Shi returned to Luojiāo after forty years. When he asked about old matters, the elders still remembered them and asked, "Are you the descendant of County Magistrate Wang who executed Bai Shen Guan with a beating?"

The Previous Life of Taoist Priest Jia In the Xuande era, Taoist Priest Jia of the Chao Yuan Temple was the son of Grand Secretary Jia of Yuerupo. He knew that his previous life was that of Wang Xiucai, a scholar from Yizhen in Luxiu. During the military campaigns of Emperor Xuanzong of the Jin Dynasty, he was captured by the northern cavalry and forced to ride away, but he escaped. The cavalry pursued him, and he was killed by a spear that hit his forehead. After death, his consciousness was not lost. Two people came to support him, showing him the various prisons, and he could not bear the terror, so they supported him out. He passed over a stone bridge and saw a lotus pot containing evil blood, and was ordered to drink it. He found it stinky, and his mouth could not approach it, so he refused to drink. The two people did not force him, but pushed him into the water. Afterward, he opened his eyes and knew he had been reborn into this family. On the third day, he was washed, and on the one-month celebration, neighbors came to congratulate, and he saw everything, but could not speak. At six or seven years old, he spoke about his previous life and asked to become a monk. His parents had no choice but to agree. He was sent to the Chao Yuan Temple to become a Taoist novice. One day, the captor came to the temple, and people told him about his previous life. The captor also clearly remembered, and there was no mistake. He looked at his forehead, and the scar still existed.

**Unusual Event of a Ten-Day Gathering** Wei Zhongyi from Dingxiang, having passed the imperial examinations as a child, became a judge in the police court of Liaoyang. He was preparing to retake the imperial examinations for poetry and prose and joined a summer course with his peers, gathering every ten days. One of the participants passed away. On another day of the ten-day gathering, the others expressed their feelings about the living and the dead, still setting up a place for the deceased. Soon after, the food arrived, and the others picked up their chopsticks, and the person setting up the place for the deceased did the same. They all cried together, losing their voices, and eventually stopped eating. **Jiyuan Temple and the Sea Pond** The Jiyuan Temple was built during the Sui dynasty. Behind the temple was a large pool, which the locals called the Sea Pond. Offerings of wine and paper money, or other items, were all thrown into this sea pond. At the end of spring each year, paper ashes rose from the bottom of the water, known as the Sea Offering. The water was also clear at times, and the objects at the bottom of the pool were clearly visible. Sometimes, wine vessels and parasols would float to the surface from the bottom of the water, known as divine gifts. Even heavy items like silver cups and incense boxes would float. Spectators stood around the water, and when the floating items reached them, they would pick them up with a long-handled sieve, bow, and leave. The wine vessels all had inscribed marks with dates and names. The wine often tasted good. **Continued Stories from the Yi Jian Zhi** **Loyal Chicken** Fang Hao Xi Bai, when serving as the governor of Luzhou, had a guest arrive and cook a chicken. The female chicken circled the house, mourning, and died after three days without eating or drinking. Many literati wrote poems and essays about the event. I named the chicken "Loyal Chicken."

### The Filial Dog of the Wang Family The fifth son of the Wang family in Huizhou, named Wuge, had a very well-behaved dog. When Wuge, who was around twelve or thirteen years old, died, the dog followed him to the burial site, where it wandered and looked around as if it had seen something. Afterward, the dog went to the tomb side every day and returned at dusk. This continued for nearly a hundred days. People called it a "filial dog." ### The Foxes and the Sawed Tree In the village of Zheng in the north of Yangqu, there lived a man named Tie Li, whose profession was catching foxes. At the end of the Dading era, one day he set up a net in an ancient tomb north of a ditch, tying a pigeon as bait. He was on a big tree waiting for the foxes. After the second watch of the night, a group of foxes arrived and spoke in human language, saying, "Tie Li, Tie Li, did you use a pigeon to trick us? Your family, father and son, are like a herd of donkeys. You don't want to farm, only learning to kill living beings. Our relatives inside and outside are all harmed by this thief. Today the fate has come to this, come down from the tree nicely. Otherwise, we will saw it down and not talk anymore!" Immediately, they heard the sound of pulling the saw. They shouted loudly, lifting the pot to boil oil, to cook this thief. The fire also started. Tie Li was afraid, not knowing what to do. Looking at his waist, he only had a big axe. Thinking if the tree falls, he would chop it randomly. Soon it became dawn, and the foxes left. The tree had no saw marks, only several cow ribs on the side. Tie Li knew that their transformations were not real. That night he went again. Before the first watch of the night, the foxes came, weeping and cursing in a reasonable manner. Tie Li hung a fire pot on his waist, took some firecrackers, secretly lit them, and threw them under the tree. The firecrackers exploded, making a loud noise. The foxes ran around in confusion. They were caught by the net, closed their eyes waiting to die, not uttering a word. He killed them with an axe.

Fish Flying in the Ji River In the year Renyin (1242), fish were seen flying in the waters of Jiyuan. Birds pecked at them, causing them to fall, and people collected and ate them, with no other strange occurrences. In the year Jia Chen (1244), at dawn without wind or clouds, several fish suddenly fell from the sky in the southwest of Anxian Town, southwest of Ma Ling. It was unknown where they came from. The fish were slightly smaller than those from Jiyuan. According to the fish farming methods of Tao Zhu Gong: "Put turtles in the pond, otherwise they will fly away." Stone Buddha Moving In the eighth year of Zhengda (1251), a stone Buddha in Tengzhou, three miles east of the city, suddenly began to move for several months. It stopped moving only after the local official died. This was reported by Zhang Zhong'an of Yuji. God Pulling Out a Tree At the end of the Xingding period (1224), a farmer in Caizhou saw a sudden rainstorm while walking in the fields. He heard voices in the air saying, "Dare you?" Then he heard a loud laugh. Half a mile away, he saw a large willow tree uprooted and thrown ten steps away. The mud was imprinted with large buttock marks, about the size of a grain basket. It was a god who uprooted the tree, sitting down in the mud, laughing loudly.

High Official’s Hidden Virtue** Gao Gongbu, a high-ranking official, had a son named Deqing. Gao’s father once served as the magistrate of Feixu and Nanhe, saving over a thousand people through his official duties. Deqing was born in this county. Forty years later, he was appointed as the military governor of An Guo Army. The elders who had witnessed his father’s deeds welcomed him with support staff, cheering at his arrival. Deqing also erected a stele in the county office to explain the reason for hidden good deeds and public rewards. Within a month, his son Song and his nephew Zhu both passed the imperial examinations. People regarded this as a great honor. **Governor Hu Exorcises the Fox Spirit** Hu Yan Gao, appointed through a recommendation for integrity as the magistrate of Jimo County in the second year of the Mingchang era, faced a problem when he arrived. The county office was located in a corner of an ancient city, occupied by a mischievous fox spirit. The fox would hide during the day and emerge at night, taking on various forms: sometimes it would appear as a prison guard, releasing prisoners; sometimes it would disguise itself as an official entertainer, stealing clothing and seducing men and women. The fox’s mischief was so severe that it even led to deaths. The people of the county had no way to deal with it and instead offered incense and sacrifices to the fox, a practice that had continued for fifty years. When Hu arrived, he learned about the situation and told his colleagues, “The official residence should be occupied by virtuous people, not by spirits!” The office had been vacant for a long time and was in a state of disrepair. Hu immediately ordered it to be repaired. The next day, he sat in the office with a lit candle, determined to confront the fox. At midnight, the fox began to howl in the garden behind the office, followed by a chorus of other foxes. Soon, they surrounded the courtyard. In the center stood a large white fox, roaring as if it were ready to attack.

In a panic, the soldiers fled in all directions, finding no place to hide. Yan Gao sat calmly and composed, and the foxes also did not advance. After a long while, they retreated. This situation lasted for three days, and then the foxes did not return. After another ten or so days, a female servant was possessed, leaping and laughing madly, as if speaking in her sleep. Yan Gao wrote in red ink and placed it in the servant's hairpin, forcing her away. On the same day, she regained her senses. The next morning, the magistrate returned from his patrol and encountered hundreds of foxes heading southeast of the county. The foxes again possessed a woman in the household of Jiang Chong, the magistrate of Dengzhou. Jiang Chong went to an island to invite a Taoist priest to perform rituals: binding the possessed woman to a wheel and burying the axle in the ground, then having people turn the wheel. After a while, the woman quickly spat out foul saliva, revealing that she was a fox from Jimo, driven away by Hu Gong. The elders of Jimo carved a stone in honor of Yan Gao, naming it "The Stone of Hu Gong Driving Away the Foxes." This was recorded by Li Zhi of Ping Mountain. Yan Gao, from Wu'an, served as the Deputy Magistrate of Fengxiang.

Poetic Prophecy of Lu Shou Lu Shou, courtesy name Xiangqing, was from Daxing. He served as the governor of Ruzhou for a month before being dismissed. On the Looking at Song Tower, he inscribed a poem: "Cherish the old mountain scenery in the tower, good to use the delicate eyebrows to serve the new official." Not long after, he passed away. People considered it a poetic prophecy. Dream of Minister Meng Minister Meng Youzhi, during the third year of the Dading era (1163 AD), passed all four examinations at the local, provincial, national, and imperial levels, all with first place, and served as a翰林 (Imperial Academy member). He served as a literary official in the Prince's mansion and sought medical treatment due to illness. After a long time, he was appointed as the deputy governor of the military and state affairs in Shanzhou. When he suffered the death of a close relative, he died from grief and sorrow. Before he passed the imperial examination, he foresaw his future path in dreams, and all of which later proved true. A neighbor named Li Sheng said: In the sixth month of the year when Meng passed away, stars fell continuously in front of the empty pavilion. A person from Bian (Kaifeng) named Gao Gongzhen, also known as Te Fu, wrote a poem in mourning: "It is said that his lifelong dreams, the future is all before him." He also said: "People lament the burial of the jade tree, heaven opens the star of literature." The poem is not very well-crafted, but it shows that in the moments of his rise and fall, life and death, the creator made him know in advance. The reason he was given a heavy reputation throughout the country, was not accidental.

The Child Prodigy Ma Ma Jiuchou, also known as Zhiji, was from Xianzhou. He recognized characters at three years old and could write cursive script at seven years old, producing large characters that could be as big as several feet. Wherever he went, he was called a prodigy. Emperor Zhang of Jin summoned him, asking, "Are you also afraid when you enter the palace?" He replied, "The relationship between ruler and subject is like that between father and son. Would a son fear his father?" The emperor found this remarkable. Since the Mingchang period, there have been five people called prodigies: Chang Tian-shou from Taiyuan, who wrote poetry at four years old with the lines, "I have a scroll of scriptures, not needing to write it down stroke by stroke"; Liu Wenrong from Hehe, who wrote poetry at six years old with the lines, "The new forms of flowers and birds, the old sky of sun and moon"; Liu Wei, who was seven years old and was ordered to compose 'The Phoenix Comes in Order'; Zhang Shijie from Xin'en, who was five or six years old and was also summoned, composing 'The Empress's White Silk Fan Painting Plum Blossoms' with the lines, "The village cannot be destroyed, moving it to the moon to plant," and later often lived in seclusion without coming out; the other three were not worthy of mention, but only Ma Jiuchou could establish himself. One day, his fame spread throughout the world, and respected elders like Xianxian Gong referred to him as a "reclusive scholar" without naming him.

陈守诚感 陈大年，字世德，是吉州人。在泰和年间担任我们州的刺史。当时正值秋季大旱，蝗虫从南向北蔓延。陈大年在石岭关进行祭祀，蝗虫便不再进入州境。有一个死囚名叫马柏儿，已经辗转多个州府调查了十三年！陈大年已经决定判处他死刑，只等签署文书。陈大年在夜晚对着星星祈祷：“决定囚犯的生死不再有疑虑，但仍然担心可能有冤情。现在旱情已经非常严重，如果囚犯确实没有冤情，明天将会下大雨；如果有冤情，那么雨就会停止。以此来占卜。”第二天果然下了大雨，于是陈大年决定释放了这个囚犯。那一年，庄稼获得了大丰收。

Yú Lìnggōng, the Early Sage Yú Lìnggōng Zhōngwén, also known as Zhìfū, composed a poem about snowflakes at the age of four, which reads: "Jade flowers and jade buds, one by one falling on the steps. Asking where the flowers come from, even the Eastern Lord does not know." He served as a chancellor in the Liao dynasty and was granted the position of Pingzhangzhengshi and the title of Puguo Gong upon returning to the Song dynasty. Spiritual Remains of Chen Xi Yi In Zhang Chaogu, Mount Hua, the spiritual remains of Chen Xi Yi are found. The mountain path is perilously steep, with no ground below. Once, Li Qinsu of Hedong visited this place. Chen's bones were large and different from those of modern people, hard, heavy, smooth, and lustrous like blue jade. The power of the Dao is evident here. His disciple's remains are also nearby, and when compared to Chen, the difference between the divine and the mortal is clear! Paintings by Mǎ Guāngchén Mǎ Guāngchén, the son of Mǎ Zhīshēn, was around ten years old when he painted landscapes with a sense of distance. He died shortly after becoming a young adult. Wáng Zǐduān, the Grand Secretary, inscribed on his paintings: "In the fine tomb, paintings and drawings lie idle. Tears in his final years, his last brush strokes across many mountains." People say that he was known for his painting at a young age and lamented by famous scholars, a rare occurrence in ancient times.

A Horse Biting the Official of Dingxiang During the Tahe period, a man surnamed Guo served as an official in Dingxiang. One day, a horse belonging to the Cheng family from the western region broke loose and charged directly into the hall, biting the official and causing him to fall. Dozens of people stood by, shouting and beating the horse, but they could not stop it. Within a short while, the horse bit the official to death. The injuries were so severe that they could not be looked at. The horse then ran out of the city and was eventually caught. Three days later, the official was buried, and the horse was tied and thrown into a fire. People said: This horse is not possessed by any spirit; it must be seeking revenge from another life. Ghost Market Pei Zhe, a翰林 from Yangwu, was six or seven years old when he was carried on his grandfather's horse to a place in the northeast of the county. Upon reaching the outer moat, he saw a market on both sides of the gate, with people and objects all about two feet tall. Men, women, the elderly, children, officials, soldiers, monks, and Taoists were bustling about. Merchants were buying and selling, with people carrying loads, riding donkeys and camels, and using carts—there was nothing they did not have. He told his grandfather about this, but his grandfather thought it was nonsense and did not believe him. However, on several occasions, he also saw the same thing. This is similar to what is recorded in Lü Shi's *Jie Shi Lu* about Zhou Ding from Wuping, who lived in a village as a child. One day, when the county people were at the market, Ding rode a long-eared donkey and went with his father to the market. At that time, the ground was slightly visible, and he saw rows of Buddha statues on both sides of the road. He closed his eyes in fear and dared not look, but when he opened his eyes again, they were gone. The two stories are very similar, but why were there so many Buddha statues?

The Dog of the Yan Family in Yuanwu In the suburbs of Yuanwu, there was a family of the Yan clan living near the city wall. The old man and his mother died during the winter of the year of Xinmao due to military chaos. The young men under the wall were captured and not given time to bury or cover up their bodies. At that time, the fields were filled with corpses, which were usually eaten by foxes and dogs, and it was impossible to distinguish who they belonged to. The Yan family's dog also ate people, but it protected the old man and his mother, fighting daily with other dogs, none of which dared to approach. This continued for over a month. Some of the Yan family's relatives escaped and returned, eventually managing to find and bury the remains. Truly, it was a filial dog! Prophecy of the Years Ancient people often spoke of a lifespan of tens of thousands of years. At the beginning of the state, the people were pure and simple, and each time they raised their cups, they only wished for a lifespan of one hundred and twenty years. The founder, Wumu, used the fifth year of Zhenghe and the fifth year of Tianqing in the year of Yiwei as the first year of the state. Until the third year of Tianxing in the reign of the Emperor Ai, when Cizhou fell, it was exactly two cycles of sixty years! The prophecy of the years was thus fulfilled.

The Omen of the Xun Gate In the eighth year of Tianhe, Liu Yu was enthroned as the Emperor of Great Qi, with Daming as his capital. The gates had old names such as Xun Gate, Anliu Gate, and Shunyu Gate, which were considered auspicious. Thus, he took the name of the gate called Fuchang as his era name. Although it was somewhat contrived, there was also a certain rationale behind it. The Peach Cup The Taoist Master Han from Gongxia, originally from a family of officials, once appointed to an official position through hereditary privilege. In his middle age, he met a strange person and gained something, which led him to resign and study the Dao. I once saw him in Longcheng, Qin Prefecture. He said during the early years of Taihe, after the autumn rain, he walked through the mountains and suddenly saw a large leaf floating downstream. Han initially did not pay much attention to it. Soon, among several leaves, a peach as large as a cup and plate appeared, but it stopped due to an obstruction by a stone. Han picked it up. The peach was red and fragrant, not something commonly seen by ordinary eyes. He knew it was a rare occurrence. He bowed twice to the Three Peaks and ate it. After finishing, he carried the branches and leaves back. He went to a high and secluded cave, drilled through the peach pit, took out the kernel, and swallowed it. It was as sweet as cream and honey. He then made two wine cups from the pit, each capable of holding more than a ladle. From then on, Han either ate or practiced fasting. At the time, he was already sixty years old, but his appearance was like that of a forty-year-old man. One day, he asked me for wine and used these cups to pour wine. The pit, when filled with wine, turned red and fresh. He asked me to compose a poem titled "The Peach Cup," but I had not yet had the time to do so. After crossing north, the Master Yin of Changchun also had a peach cup, which he said was from the inner palace during the Xuan and Zheng reigns.

### The Drowned Ghost In Ze Prefecture, there was a needleworker. One day, after the evening meal, he was arranging his needles when he heard someone coming up the moat. He laughed and said, "Tomorrow I will get a replacement!" Someone asked who the replacement would be. He replied, "A messenger from Zhen Ding carrying an umbrella and a book folder is coming to bathe in the moat. I will get a replacement." The needleworker went out to look but saw nothing, realizing it was a ghost. The next day, he stood at the door waiting for him. After breakfast, a messenger left his umbrella and book folder at the needleworker's house, saying he wanted to go bathe in the moat. The needleworker asked where he was from, and he replied that he was from Zhen Ding. The needleworker then told the messenger that there was a bathhouse in the city and offered to pay for a back-scrubbing. The messenger asked why, and the needleworker told him what he had heard the previous day. The messenger refused and left after several thanks. That night, after the second watch, someone threw stones and tiles at the door, cursing, "I worked hard to get this replacement, but this thief ruined it for me. I swear I will drag you into the water!" The next morning, the needleworker found a pile of stones and tiles. This continued for several nights. The person moved to another house to avoid the ghost. This story is told in the village of Jiaofu near Secret Water. ### The Ghost Woman of the School in Di Prefecture Wang Zhongze, the Right Censor, lived in a school in Di Prefecture when he was young. The cook reported, "There is a ghost woman who comes every night to disturb us, and we cannot sleep." Wang said, "If she comes again tonight, grab her clothes and shout, and we will go see what is happening." That night, she indeed came. The person grabbed her arm and shouted. The students took a lantern and went to see, and they found an ancient coffin board. They burned it, and the ghost disappeared. This story is told by Wang Zhongze.

**汤盘周鼎** An Chang, courtesy name Shunzhi, studied seal script under Party Chengzhi and was well-versed in ancient characters. At the end of the Taihe period, he once saw a white jade square vessel in the imperial treasury, nearly four small inches in size, with a base inscription of nine characters: "Virtue is renewed day by day, and day after day it is renewed." Emperor Zhangzong ordered him to authenticate it. There was also a square bronze vessel with two ears and four feet, adorned with a饕餮 pattern on the thunder motif. The inscription read: "Duke Lu made a ritual bronze vessel for King Wen." The bronze was ancient and polished to a jade-like luster, no longer showing its metallic nature. **莲十三花** Kang Liangfu recounted that in Guantai, Cizhou, during the Cheng'an period, a lotus flower with thirteen petals bloomed in a pond. That year, Liu Gui passed the imperial examination. He eventually became the magistrate of Jingzhao, the inspector of judicial affairs. **瑞禾** In Taizi Village, Guo County, Fengxiang, in the Gengzi year, Hao Shi's eighty-acre field produced a plant where each leaf had a small ear, and there were twelve more, making a total of thirteen. When one stalk was harvested and processed, it yielded ten liters of grain. The next year, Hao Shi was appointed to lead ten thousand troops and wore the golden tiger talisman. A subordinate general, Li Zao, had once seen an ancient plant with nine ears on one stem, but it was not as numerous as this.

### 黄真人 In the region of Xiuwu, there was a man named Zhang Zhi, whose courtesy name was Junmian. His father, Zhonghe, served as a low-ranking official in his youth and was fond of praying for immortality. One day, Huang Fanchuo descended and stayed with him, and he was called Huang Zhenren. They hung a painting of Huang Zhenren in front of their home and prayed for everything. In the second year of Chongqing, Junmian took the imperial examination. Zhonghe asked, "Son, have you heard the emperor's question for the exam?" Junmian replied, "The heavenly mechanism cannot be revealed!" After the exam, when Zhonghe asked again, Junmian wrote, "I composed a poem about the loyal ministers supporting the emperor and the virtuous deeds recorded in the *Taichang* (a historical text)." Zhonghe then asked, "Did you pass the exam?" Junmian replied, "Yellow robe on top, green robe on bottom." Zhonghe did not understand and asked for an explanation, to which Junmian wrote, "The heavenly mechanism cannot be revealed!" By April, when the results were to be announced, Zhonghe asked, "The list will soon be released, please tell me in advance!" Junmian wrote, "Green robe, six robes, is it not your name, Junmian?" When the list was finally released, Huang Jifu was indeed the first place winner, while Junmian's name was at the very bottom. There were many such instances. There were also humorous poems that were quite amusing. ### 摩利天咒 In Xinzhou, there was a judge named Liu Junpan.
