In the early years of Zhenyou, he heard that there was military activity in the northern regions. He began reciting the *Molixian Tianzuo* (a specific incantation) at home. When the city was captured, all twenty-five members of his family were spared from the war. Only one servant did not believe in the incantation and started reciting it only when the city was under siege. He was captured for several days but managed to escape back. After the Southern Migration, he settled in Yongning and continued to recite the incantation. The literatus Xue Manqing recorded this event.

Wang Sǒu's Unseen Virtue In Songzhuang, a village in Rang County, there lived an elderly man named Wang Sǒu, who was known as Wang Pingshi. He was eighty-one years old, his wife was eighty, they had four sons, and more than twenty grandchildren, including great-grandchildren who had already married. From Wang Sǒu down to his great-grandchildren, there were thirty-six households, all of whom had been married for a long time, and they were recommended as the first household in the county. His fourth son, Rong, became a military officer in the Lushan region of the Xuanwu Army due to his military achievements; his eldest grandson passed the military examination and became a patrol officer in a certain state. In front of the four districts of Songzhuang, there stood a large locust tree that was several hundred years old, with a trunk that had withered, but five branches growing inward, each forming a large tree, providing shade for dozens of steps. When I was stationed in Zhenping, I once visited his home and found him to be as healthy as someone in their sixties. People said that such health must be due to his unseen virtue, but when I asked him, he did not respond. An elderly man beside me said, "Although Wang Pingshi is old, he makes a living by treating illnesses. When a sick family invites him, he goes on horseback without bringing servants; when prescribing medicine, if one ingredient is missing, he does not mix the medicine himself; he treats the sick in poor families, visiting two or three times a day in the summer without tiring, and after the illness is cured, he does not ask for a single coin. Is this not unseen virtue?" Wang Sǒu then agreed to speak for himself, saying, "Nowadays, merchants from areas outside Lintao Mountain come with camels or donkeys carrying ginseng, which can be covered in dust and filth. After unloading them at the medicine shop, they immediately cut and give them to others. Whenever I use this herb, I must wash it with water, dry it according to the proper method, and then prepare the medicine. The same is true for other herbs. I do not dare to claim that I do good deeds for their own sake, but I cannot help but do so because it is what my heart finds at peace with." I offered him wine and said, "This gentleman's unseen virtue is great indeed!"

The Poem of Feng Fu After falling ill, Feng, a farmer from Xian'an in Wuan County, suddenly recited a poem: "South of the city, pavilions and halls line the banks of the Pu River; the wild colors and forest light are truly authentic. My eyes are filled with mist and clouds, far from the fairyland; a stream flows with flowers and trees, like the spring of Wu Ling." He died of illness at the end of the Taihe era. This was said by Hu Guorui. The Turtle in the Stone Li Lianshi, a Taoist priest from Heshun and a resident of the Jinnmen Gate, was once summoned by the Zhaofang Temple to oversee the Tianshang Temple. Throughout his life, he performed many miraculous feats, such as the "Golden Bowl Emerging from Water," among eighty-one such events. These were painted on the walls of the Shenzhao Palace in Xingzhou. His disciple Wang Shouzhong wished to engrave them on a stele for posterity. When a craftsman began to carve the stone, a turtle was found inside. The turtle spent days at the desk, becoming tame and familiar. For a hundred days, it remained there. Then, as the wind passed, it disappeared. This was said by Wang Anqing from Wuan County.

A Baby from a Stone During the Tahe period, Longge Yu Jiqi heard from Xue Ziran, a hermit on Mount Hua, that a stone mason broke open a stone on Mount Hua, and a baby emerged from the stone, quickly diving into the water. Gao the Supervisor Pays His Debt Gao, the supervisor of Suiping Douzhen Town, arrived in Douzhen and approached the wealthy Gao family to request a succession. The Gao family were simple and honest farmers, and they fell into Gao's scheme. He borrowed hundreds of strings of cash worth of wheat, and later used various deceitful tactics to avoid paying a single coin. Soon after, Gao the supervisor died, and a red calf was born. The white hair on the calf's belly formed words that read, "Debtor Gao the Supervisor." At that time, Wu Chengke, a former official from Wuzhou who had been demoted to the position of Fu Zhi Cheng, was living in this town and wrote a document titled "The Cattle's Retribution." Fan Yuanzhi Resolves a Cattle Dispute Fan Yuanzhi, the magistrate of Pingyu, heard about a dispute involving cows in the village of Hantou. The Peng Li family, who were both wealthy, had a water buffalo named Peng Li San that gave birth to a calf, which died after a few days and was thrown into the water. A neighboring Zhang family's water buffalo also gave birth to a calf. Peng Li San, lured by a herder boy, stole the Zhang family's calf and had his own water buffalo nurse it. The Zhang family beat the herder boy, and he accused the Zhang family, saying, "Peng Li San's calf died and was thrown into the water; the calf being nursed now is yours! You should report this to the authorities, and I will testify against you." The Zhang family reported the matter to the authorities. Fan Yuanzhi said, "This is not difficult." He ordered two new pots of water to be fetched and pricked the tips of the ears of the two water buffaloes, allowing their blood to drip into the water. The two streams of blood did not mix. He then pricked the calf and let its blood drip into the water, and it mixed with the Zhang family's buffalo's blood and coagulated. Immediately, he returned the calf to the Zhang family. The county praised him as a god-like judge. Fan Yuanzhi's name was Tianbao, and he was from Cizhou. The Jinshi Zhao Gongxiang personally witnessed this event.

**Carving Wood by the Old Man Jia** In Pingyang, there was an old man named Jia who, despite being blind, could carve divine images. People referred to him as "appointed to serve the imperial court." In the temple of Jiaocheng County, there was a Buddha statue carved by him, which was graceful and dignified. The monk said that Jia first felt and probed the wooden frame, then, having a sudden insight, he swung his axe like the wind. I recall the blind monk of Zhaozhou, who could spit ink to paint and sprinkle colors on top, also spitting them. At the home of a magistrate named Mao, there was a tiger crouching under a large tree, with a green panther lying beside it. The tiger's eyes shone like gold, and looking at it made one's hair stand on end. Even Zhaozhou's monk was not beyond this. The Buddhist concept of the six sense organs functioning interchangeably, perhaps this is how they progress. **The Mediumistic Communication of Yan Da** In Rang County, there was a farmer named Yan Da. During the reign of Emperor Chengzong, he went with his fellow villager Liu Jin to Shangluo to buy cattle, but Yan died of illness. Liu Jin wrote a letter to Yan's family. Yan's mother and wife performed worship and offerings in front of their home, and a gust of wind blew the ashes of the paper towards the southwest village. This village was where Yan's young wife lived, about five or six li away. Soon after, someone came to report that Yan Da spoke through his wife, wanting to meet his mother and wife. His mother and wife rushed to the village, embraced him, and cried, asking, "Why did you die?" He replied, "I died by fate, but I was deceived by Liu Jin. I told you this beforehand. What was the price of a certain cow, using silk; what was the price of another cow, using silver; he took advantage of my death without evidence, wanting to deceive and defraud me!" The prices of gold and silver were all recorded in writing.

A ghost complained, "This person's emotions and reason cannot be endured. I am already dead, and he has cloth, which he used to cover my face with a bandage." A butcher standing nearby said, "You owe me a certain amount of money for meat. Your family, because you are dead, no longer repays me. Now I am making them repay me." Yan bowed his head for a long time, then looked up at the butcher and said, "I am already dead, so what more do I care about?" The onlookers laughed. Later, Liu Jin returned home. People told him, "Earlier, it was said that Yan was very powerful, and he first informed his family about the debt!" When Liu Jin saw his mother, he did not dare to cheat by even a single coin. Zhiyuan and Yan were from the same village, and he explained the matter to him. This is the same as the case of the tattooed soldier Shi Gui during the Zhengda period. After Shi Gui's death, a man appeared to collect the debt from the officials of Wuyang County, and the matter was reported to the Minister of Agriculture, Zhang Gong. The minister found the matter unusual and ordered his subordinate, Wang Zhongkuan, to handle it. Shi Gui had written evidence to prove the debt, and it was immediately ordered to be repaid.

Immortal Elixir for Longevity and Food Abstinence A single pill of the Immortal Elixir for Longevity and Food Abstinence allows one to abstain from food for life. One catty of bright cinnabar, processed and purified; one catty of alum, burned until it turns yellow; one catty of white茯苓 as white as snow, or optionally half a catty more; one catty of yellow oxide of lead, light red in color, processed and purified; seven and a half taels of frankincense, weighed by scale; three taels of mercury; thirty sheets of large gold leaf; one catty of white honey; two catties of purified wax. Each ingredient should be carefully selected. First, grind the alum in a mortar until it is fine, then add the mercury and grind further until no mercury specks remain. Next, add the yellow oxide of lead, cinnabar, and gold leaf, and grind again. Then, add the powdered茯苓 and frankincense, and grind until all are thoroughly mixed. Place the mixture in a jade bowl and set it over a warm pot, ensuring the water does not cool. Separately, melt the honey and wax in a pan, mix them together, and combine with the mixture using a wooden spoon. Form the pills by hand, with each catty making twelve pills, ensuring there are no seams. Optionally, coat the pills with either cinnabar or mercury, or leave them uncoated. If coating with mercury, take three or two grains of mercury, rub them with saliva in your palm to make a paste, and mix with three or five pills to coat them. During preparation, avoid contact with chickens, dogs, and women. Once the elixir is prepared, store it in a jade container. If one wishes to abstain from food, first prepare a meal of three catties of oil, one catty of wax, one catty of white flour, and one catty of honey, making a pancake or flatbread. If unavailable, plain rice or porridge can be eaten instead. One must eat until full, then take the elixir. Take one pill with a decoction of frankincense.

After another hour or so, take white flour and fry it until cooked, then make it into pills using honey wax, each as large as a paulownia seed, and take one hundred pills at a time with warm white soup or frankincense soup. This is called the "later medicine." Having already eaten a full meal, one then takes this later medicine, so for two or three days one does not feel weak. Even if one does feel tired, it causes no harm. After taking the medicine, one should remain unaffected by worldly distractions. When the mind is agitated, qi disperses; when one speaks too much, qi is harmed. Therefore, those practicing辟谷 (fasting) should take calming the mind and nurturing qi as their foundation. Respond to events as they come, but do not dwell on them after they pass. Frequently swallow air while facing the sun as a means of replenishment. Tea and soup may be consumed freely, but one should not eat foods that leave residue in the body. Avoid anger and avoid excessive labor. After ten days, though the body may be lean, the tendons and bones will become light and strong, and the spirit will become clear and open. If one wishes to resume eating, it must be after fourteen days, waiting until the medicine has reached the dan tian. Only then may one begin to eat again. If one eats before fourteen days have passed, the medicine will descend along with the internal organs. After resuming eating, if one wishes to stop again, it is not necessary to take medicine; simply use frankincense soup to balance the process. In years of famine, when even fathers and sons or husbands and wives are driven to cannibalism, take the flesh, pound it into a paste, make it into a pill the size of a bullet, coat it with yellow cinnabar, place it in a paper strip, and sew it onto the navel, securing it with a wrap-around garment. Whenever an arrowhead has not yet been expelled, apply it as described above, then use powdered ivory to rub on the wound. If the arrow has been embedded for a long time, use a sharp blade or needle to slightly puncture the skin, then apply the ivory powder, and the arrowhead will come out by itself. Whether it is a fish bone stuck in the throat, a needle, a coin, or a wheat awn, regardless of how long it has been lodged there, this method is effective.

Rescuing from Smoke Death In the winter of the year Xinhui, during the reign of Emperor Ling, a cave in the southwest of Dexing was inhabited by residents who had sought refuge there to escape the soldiers. The soldiers attacked, and all five hundred people inside the cave were killed by smoke. Among them was a leader named Li, who, in a state of confusion, managed to find a frozen turnip. He chewed it, and as soon as he swallowed the juice, he revived. He then shared it with his brother, who also survived. As a result, all five hundred people were saved. The turnip, a small and seemingly insignificant object, had the power to save lives! A ship lost in midstream is worth a thousand gold pieces—this is no empty saying! Zhao Caiqing from the River region added, "Carbon smoke can often be fatal. Before going to bed, cut a piece of turnip and place it in the fire; the smoke will not be harmful. If you don't have turnips, dry and grind them in advance for emergency use, and they will also be effective."

**Divine Formula:** **Astragalus Powder.** Treats bone steaming, transmitted corpse labor, alternating chills and fever, exhaustion, and coughing: three money of *Asafoetida*; chop a handful of *Artemisia* and finely cut it; a handful of northeastern *Peach branches* and finely chop them; *Licorice* the size of the patient's middle finger, left for men and right for women; two and a half liters of a child's urine. First soak the medicine overnight with the urine, the next morning boil and extract one liter, take it warm on an empty stomach, divided into three doses. The next dose should be mixed with three money of *Areca nut* powder. After walking ten li, take another dose. If a man is ill, a woman should prepare the medicine; if a woman is ill, a man should prepare the medicine. During preparation, avoid those in mourning, pregnant women, the sick, and unclean things or animals. After taking the medicine, avoid greasy, damp, and cold foods. After taking one or two doses, the patient may vomit worms or experience diarrhea, and no further medication is needed. If there is no vomiting or diarrhea, the patient should take the full dose. If the disease is above, it will cause vomiting; if below, it will cause diarrhea. The worms will look like horse tails or human hair, and the patient will then recover. Treating such diseases typically takes months or years, but only this formula was divinely granted and works instantly.

Liu Yu, a scholar from Lingchuan and a graduate of the imperial examinations with the courtesy name Binshu, obtained the prescription from Yan Lang Zhi, who said it was an ancient prescription from the Cui family. Yan first suffered from this disease, nearly dying, but was cured by the prescription. Liu used it to treat an official courtesan from Ningzhou, benefiting from three to four sheng of inch-long white worms shaped like scallion roots, and she immediately recovered. After taking the medicine, all diseases were eliminated, and the five zang organs were weak and the spirit was unsettled. Immediately, White Poria Decoction was used to tonify it: one qian of white Poria; one qian of Poria cocos; three qian of ginseng; three qian of Polygala, with the heart removed; two qian of dragon bone; two qian of windproof; three qian of licorice; four qian of Ophiopogon, with the heart removed; five qian of rhinoceros horn, ground into powder. Fresh dried rehmannia four qian; seven large jujubes; two large sheng of water, decocted to make eight fen. Divided into three doses, taken warm. After walking about five li, take another dose. Still avoid wind and cold. If not feeling well, prepare another dose the next day. The above two medicines must be taken continuously. According to Hao Wen's note, this formula originally comes from the *Puji Jiajian Fang*. The language is concise, and it does not specify the origin, so people do not take it very seriously, hence a detailed discussion is provided.

Two Remedies for Boils on the Back For treating boils on the back, brain abscesses, and all kinds of bad sores: At the initial stage, pick a single stalk of Xanthium, finely chop the leaves and seeds, and do not use iron tools. Use a clay pot to boil water for two large bowls, boiling until half done. If the sore is in the upper part of the body, take it slowly after a meal, and if it causes vomiting, wait until the vomiting stops before taking more, until all is consumed. If the sore is in the lower part of the body, take it on an empty stomach. The sore will break open by itself and discharge pus, and it will no longer spread. Apply a separate medicinal ointment to the sore. This remedy was displayed in the family of Zhang Boyu from the capital region to pass down to descendants, and all his later generations passed the imperial examinations. People say it was a reward for good deeds. For treating all kinds of bad sores, take the Trichosanthes remedy: Use one piece of Trichosanthes, remove the skin, and use the pulp and seeds; four ounces of ginger; two ounces of licorice, preferably with horizontal veins, finely chopped and used raw; one bowl of alcohol without ash, boil until half done, and take it concentrated. Do not use copper or iron during the boiling process. If the disease is in the upper part of the body, take it after a meal; if in the lower part, take it on an empty stomach.

Seeing the formulas of Hong Shi and Chen Rihua. Originally obtained in the Central Plains from Zhang Linqing, the Ministry of Revenue official. The formula includes the addition of rhubarb, or costus root, or frankincense, or myrrh. Generally, it uses Trichosanthes fruit, ginger, and licorice as the main ingredients. For skin diseases, first use laxatives, then apply the Trichosanthes formula. Daily, take three to five qian of frankincense and mung bean powder, to prevent toxins from entering the abdomen. Externally, apply ointment, and the patient need not worry. At the age of twenty-one, I served my father in Longcheng. In the spring of Da'an, the year Gengwu, my father developed a boil on his temple. I was young and foolish, usually occupied with the studies of a scholar, and knew nothing about medicine. Surrounded by quacks, I let them do as they pleased, and my father ultimately passed away due to this. After returning home, I obtained this formula from the family school and used it to treat others, achieving a hundredfold effectiveness. Reflecting on the past, I feel deep regret and shame. As a son, not knowing medicine led to such a disaster! Therefore, I record this as a warning.

**内藏库龙** In the fifth month of the fifth year of the Shenchu era, a black dragon appeared at the Yang Water of Zhaoli Mountain. The founder of the Liao Dynasty, Yelü Abaoji, rushed to the location. After three days, he finally arrived, and the dragon was still present. Yelü Abaoji shot and killed the dragon. The dragon had one horn, a long tail, short feet, a body five chi long, and a tongue two and a half cun long. He ordered it to be stored in the imperial treasury. It remained there even after the Jin Dynasty moved its capital southward in the Zhenyou era, and people saw its tongue in the shape of straw. **都城夜怪** My maternal uncle Zhang Boda and Zeng Feiqing, who were preparing for the provincial examination in the second month of the Chongqing era, stayed at the Welcome Dragon Street. One night, Wang Xing, the servant, got up to relieve himself. As soon as his feet stepped out the door, he saw a ghost on the opposite street. The ghost had a green face and red hair, eyes like torches, and was wielding a sword on the wrist. Beside him stood a soldier who was especially terrifying and fierce. Wang Xing screamed and fell down. After about three or four hours, he woke up. When asked, he said what he had seen.

**Great Fire in the Capital City** At the end of the Da'an era, the capital city was repeatedly struck by great fires. In every household that was burned, there were often red inscriptions on the walls, which were followed by the outbreak of fire. When people asked each other about these inscriptions, they found that they were all true. The fires spread to burn several ten thousand households. There was a Buddhist pavilion in the marketplace, which had existed since the Tang dynasty. The Liao people called it the National Protection King Buddha Altar, the Thousand-Handed and Thousand-Eyed Great Compassion Pavilion, and it was written by Yu Shinan. When the pavilion was burned down, Emperor Wanyan Yong issued an order to save Yu Shinan's calligraphy, but it was already too late! Those who understood the situation said that the burning of the National Protection King Buddha Altar was extremely ominous! Within a year, there was a coup d'état by the tiger bandits. **Twin Fetuses** During the Xingding and Yuanguang eras, Wang Fengxian, a teacher at a primary school in Yangzhai, his wife first gave birth to four sons, and then to three more sons. In November of the Xinwei year, Fan Ban, a farmer from Xiurong's Fuchan Temple, his wife gave birth to three boys and three girls for three consecutive years, all of whom died. This year, she gave birth to one more boy and one more girl. Her mother-in-law, standing by, lamented, "You will surely not survive. It would be better if you could pass away early." The child suddenly spoke, saying, "I won't go! I won't go!" The mother was shocked and told her husband; before she finished speaking, the child repeated, "I won't go!" Soon after, both the boy and the girl died. In the Southern Qi dynasty, Zhu Cheng, the Grand Secretary, wrote in his *Medical Discourse* about the formation of life: "When both yin and yang come together, it is neither male nor female; when essence and blood are scattered and divided, it is a sign of twin fetuses. If one says it is transmigration, there is indeed such a principle, and there is no need to be surprised." I believe that Zhu Cheng's theory cannot be discarded, but such cases of twin fetuses are not commonly seen in the world.

Tong Ge After the Southern Migration, there was a monk in the capital who served a divine child. The child claimed to be from a noble family, surnamed Abuhuan. At eight years old, he was abandoned by Pingzhang Jinchong, the chief minister, as they fled the capital. People carried him, forcing their way out through the gates, where he was trampled to death by horses and people. He had owed the monk countless debts from past lives and now came to repay them. Noble families in the capital welcomed him, and he even conveyed messages to the imperial palace. Those who asked questions burned incense and poured wine, while the monk stood by with folded hands. The child spoke to people on his own, clearly and distinctly. Seeking his voice in the air, when the wine was poured on the ground, he descended swiftly, as if drinking it. He answered questions about fugitives and lost items, but not those involving lawsuits or interests. When asked about matters far away, he would say, "I will go and ask." After a long time, he would return, always speaking of poverty and hardship. The families he had passed through, even if the monk was not there later, he still came to speak. The monk thus became wealthy. After the fall of Kaifeng, he was heard to return to the north, entering and leaving the homes of the nobility and the court. Two-Headed Child In the twelfth month of the Xinmao year of Zhengda, a servant in the household of Wang Zisi, a scholar from Yangdi, gave birth to a child with two heads. The wet nurse thought this was strange and removed one head, but the child's breath divided into two branches and exited. In the following January, the western expeditionary forces suffered a defeat at the Three Peaks.

### Fate of Birth and Death Wang Yousi, known as Zhongze, recognized Guideyi Wu Bian Aoli Guanglu, a military officer and high-ranking official from Qingzhou. His descendants, including his son, grandson, and great-grandson, all males and females, were born in September, totaling sixteen people. Li Angxiao's fellow student Liu Yuanzhi, from Yan, had five brothers all born in July. The grandfather, father, and mother of Yan Zishou from Gaotang all died on June 29th, and Zishou also died on the same date. Could this be by chance? **The Unusual Birth and Death of Party Censuring Director** Party Gong, the Director of the Secretariat, was conceived when his mother dreamed that the Tang Taoist Wu Jun came to lodge. He had a well-groomed and dignified appearance, looking like a deity. He served in the Western Office for thirty years and retired as the Director of the Secretariat. He passed away at home on the eighteenth day of the ninth month in the third year of Da'an. That night, a large star fell in his residence. His seal script and clerical script reached a divine level; after Li Yangbing, he was the only one! He once said that the Tang people Han and Cai did not understand the art of writing, and the eight-part script comes from the seal and clerical scripts. Therefore, his writing surpassed Zhong and Cai, and those below are not worth mentioning. His small regular script was like Yu and Zhu, and it should also be the first in the imperial court. Calligraphy takes Lu Gong as the standard, and those below Liu Chengxuan are not worth mentioning. Ancient people named their arts, but he alone possessed all of them. Can we not call him complete? He was so highly esteemed by his contemporaries. Dongpo said about Han Tuozhi, "life has its own origin, and death has its own purpose"; observing his life and death, one can also be without shame in these words.

Lady Bestowed by Heaven The temple of the public deity in the mountain of the town in Guangning is renowned for its miraculous responses. The statues within are fearsome and ugly, with trees obscuring and shadowing the area. Even during the day, people who enter the temple feel terrified and their hair stands on end. Those nearby say that at quiet nights, one can hear the sound of pursuit or plunder. Thus, passersby may take detours to avoid it. Liang Gong Su, a high-ranking official, lived in this village on Qianma Ridge. When he was a scholar preparing for the imperial examinations, he formed a study group with other scholars during the summer and discussed matters of ghosts and spirits. They listed contemporary people known for their courage, but Liang Gong did not agree with them. He then said, "I can enter the temple of the mountain of the town at dusk or during a gloomy, overcast time and walk around the corridors once." The other scholars urged him, saying, "If you can go, how can we believe you?" Liang Gong replied, "I will mark the places I walk with something, using this as proof." The next evening, they agreed to go together. The other scholars waited outside the temple gate. He waved his sleeves and went straight in. He marked the eastern corner of the temple, and as he felt around, he found a person leaning against the wall. Liang Gong thought it was a ghost and carried it out. The other scholars greeted him and asked what he saw. He laughed and said, "I carried a ghost with me! You can light a fire to see it."

When the fire arrived, they saw a beautiful woman whose attire was completely different from that of ordinary people. They wanted to question her, but she was breathing weakly, as if she were in a stupor. The students truly believed her to be a ghostly entity and stood in a circle around her, guarding her. After a long time, she opened her eyes and saw people surrounding her, and she was terrified and could not control herself. They asked her where she was, and the students told her the place and that they had found her in the temple. They also questioned her about whether she was a human or a ghost, and where she had come from. She replied, "I am a woman from a prominent family in Yangzhou, who was escorted to my husband's home on an auspicious day. In the carriage, I was suddenly carried away by a strong wind, and my senses became scattered; I do not know how I came to be here." The students were delighted and said, "Liang has not yet married, and this divine being has sent a wife to him from Yangzhou. Truly, there is a fate at work here! We can make this work." Liang then took the woman back with him. Soon after, he passed the imperial examination and was promoted to an official position. Within a few years, he achieved a high and prominent position, and the woman gave birth to several children. Therefore, people at the time gave her the title "Heavenly Bestowed Lady," and she even conveyed messages to the imperial palace. Liang, in the year of Dading, governed with virtue, and many of the elderly still saw him. After the war, the Liang family remained numerous, and when people asked about their family background, they often mentioned the descendants of "Heavenly Bestowed Lady."

**The North-facing King** Liang Gong Su, when he was a candidate for office, sought immortals to ask about his future. The immortals wrote: "Enter the position of chancellor at the age of sixty." Later, he became the military governor of Zhengde at the age of sixty, but the prophecy of becoming a chancellor had not yet been fulfilled. When Emperor Shizong was angry that the Song people took the national letter from the imperial post, he selected someone from the court who could serve as a detailed inquiry envoy and not disgrace the king's command. The chancellor recommended Liang Gong Su, who was then appointed as a deputy prime minister. Upon his return, he met the emperor's expectations and was appointed as a deputy prime minister. The prophecy of becoming a chancellor was fulfilled in this way. The Inner Courtian Yan Zixiu recorded this, and Liang Gong Su said two days before his death: "God summoned me to be the North-facing King." He then died. **Liu Zheng's Pure Filial Piety** Liu Zheng, a man from Mingzhou, was a noble person from a young age. His mother was blind, and Liu Zheng licked her eyes, and after ten days, her sight returned. When she fell ill, Liu Zheng administered medicine to her day and night, never removing his clothes, and cut meat from his thigh to feed her, doing so multiple times. After his mother's death, he carried earth to build her grave, and birds mourned and gathered around the tomb tree. He built a hut beside the grave and stayed there until the mourning period ended. The local official reported this to the emperor, who praised Liu Zheng and appointed him as the Chief of Wine for the Crown Prince. His story is recorded in the historical annals.

Chapter Three Elan Dongwei The Taoist priest Yang Gu, courtesy name Dongwei, was from Dai Prefecture and lived in seclusion on Mount Hua. He was a man of noble appearance and outstanding spiritual cultivation, never having conflicted with others throughout his life. Proficient in the *Zhuangzi* and the *Yijing*, he was known as "Mr. Yang of the *Zhuangzi*" by the world. During the reign of Emperor Shengzong (Mingchang era), he was summoned by imperial decree as a highly accomplished Taoist and assigned to the Tianshang Temple. Not long after, he returned to the mountains. On his way back, he ate with the head of the temple, Hou Sheng, in the marketplace and wrote several "fire" characters on the dining table. He then instructed Hou to say, "Yesterday, I passed through Wozhou and heard that your mother was ill. You should return quickly." Hou took leave and went home. By the time he arrived in Wozhou, his mother was not sick. Hou, in frustration, said, "Did he deceive me?" Upon returning north, he found that the Tianshang Temple had already been burned down. Once, while traveling with a guest on White Turtle Spring at Mount Song, he saw a stone crab emerge. The guest remarked, "Crabs walk sideways; is this their nature?" Elan replied, "This creature indeed walks sideways, but it is a pity that it does not encounter a righteous person!" As he pointed to the crab, it immediately walked straight. Later, he grew fond of Aizhongfang and chose to settle there. Aizhongfang previously lacked a spring, making it difficult to fetch water from a distance. Elan said, "The mountain is so beautiful; it should not lack a spring." He then purified himself through fasting and washing, offered prayers, consulted the oracle, and received a favorable sign.

At this time in October, the sunflowers near the hermitage were luxuriant and flourishing. Yang Dongwei remarked, "In classical texts, 'grass chrysanthemum' is often used interchangeably with 'sunflower,' which is likely a sign of water!" He and other Taoist priests went to search for it. They saw a faint moisture in the grass and trees at the southeastern corner, and upon digging, they indeed found a spring that could supply water for several hundred people. However, it was separated by a deep ravine to the east and limited by a range of mountains to the southeast, with a steep and rugged stone wall hundreds of steps high, making it impossible to cross. Yang Dongwei and his disciple Lu Ze, along with others, built a suspension bridge along the cliff face to facilitate passage. People were concerned that the wooden planks of the suspension bridge would rot and cause accidents, so they carved holes in the stone and embedded them to create a stone bridge, lining the spring with stone to form a pool. From then on, obtaining water became much easier. Today, people call it the "Yang Gong Spring." Xianxian once wrote an essay to commemorate this event. He also said, "My friend Pan Ruojing, courtesy name Qingrong, is a man of virtue and learning. He once accompanied Yang Dongwei on a journey and greatly admired him. He said, 'Yang Dongwei should be sought among the ancients!'" Afterward, Xianxian passed through Huazhou and recalled Yang Dongwei, saying, "Last year, I stayed at the Cloudy Terrace. I knew there was a master in Mount Hua. Today, he seems to have descended from the peak of White Cloud Peak, as if riding a crane down to the mortal world." This is how the master was praised.

Shadow Measurement** The practice of measuring shadows for astronomical observations shows that the measurements for the winter and summer solstices gradually increase in areas north of the capital. In the capital, the winter solstice shadow measures one zhang five chi seven cun six fen, and the summer solstice shadow measures two chi two cun six fen; the day is divided into 61 time units (ke) for daylight and 39 for nighttime. The high-altitude area of Jinlian Plain, located in the northwest of the capital, is about four hundred li away and is the highest place. During the summer solstice, the daylight lasts 63 time units and the nighttime 37 time units. Linhuangfu, the northern capital, is about three thousand li north of the capital, and during the summer solstice, the daylight lasts 64 time units and the nighttime 36 time units. This is recorded in the "Jie Shi Lu" (碣石录) by Lu Shi. **Hunting Dogs** In the fifth year of the Taihe era, Emperor Xuanzong hunted in Yunlong River. Hunting dogs from Xingzhou were presented by a member of the imperial family named Yaotu, who said, "These can be used for hunting tigers." The emperor then ordered a test of the dogs' abilities. When the dogs saw a tiger, one dog led the charge and lured the tiger, which chased after it. The other dogs then joined the chase, attacking from various angles. The tiger struggled to maneuver and, in anger, jumped one or two zhang, attempting to escape, but the dogs continued to pursue it. The tiger became exhausted and lay down, and the guards shot it. Ultimately, the tiger was killed by the dogs.

**Thunder Strikes the Tenant Farmer** In the village of Lu, Shaanxi, Zhang Hai had a dispute with a fellow villager, a farmer. The tenant farmer hatched a plot and falsely accused the farmer of burning the wheat stack. Zhang Hai, acting as a witness, bound the farmer and took him to the county magistrate's office. The farmer, being simple and honest, could not defend himself and was sentenced to death! As the three of them walked to the south gate of the city, suddenly, a bolt of lightning struck the tenant farmer, causing him to fall from the sky, his bones and flesh reduced to nothing, leaving only his skin and hair intact! The scholar Ni Shuyu witnessed this event. At that time, Guo Jingshu was the magistrate of Shaanxi. **The Former Life of Venerable Fang** Venerable Fang, known as Yifang, was a monk at Danxia Temple in Weishi. His former life was as Liu Xia'er, also a native of the county. In the early years of the Dading era, a group of ruffians gathered to plan the burning of the three gates of Xiangguo Temple and to seize the military treasury during the chaos. There were fifty people in total, divided into groups to investigate names. Liu Xia'er and one other person were assigned to set the fire. First, they went down to the gate to survey the area and plan how to ignite the fire. Liu Xia'er thought to himself, "This gate was built with national resources, as large as a wooden mountain. Once it is burned, it cannot be rebuilt. Such a great achievement, I am destroying it, how regrettable, how regrettable!" As he was reflecting, he was captured on the bridge in the city, interrogated and beaten to death. After his death, he was reborn into the Chen family in the county. At the age of six or seven, he could recount the events of his previous life. He found his parents, wife, and the location of their property, and there was no doubt that he was indeed Liu Xia'er. The family provided for him. He left home and entered the monastic life at Fayan Temple, later succeeding the teachings of Venerable Zhuang. He resided at Danxia Temple and personally told me this story.

The Reincarnation of Old Zhao In the year Da'an 2, Zhao San, the father of Zhao Jiu, who lived in the Caojia Alley of Gongzhou, fell gravely ill. Soon after, he was reborn in the Silver Sun family in the West Small Street of Lintao. At the age of sixteen, the boy entrusted someone to visit Zhao Jiu and told him about his past and present lives, calling Zhao Jiu to come and see. Zhao Jiu sent someone to welcome the boy. As the boy was about to leave Gongzhou, his family rushed to meet him. Zhao Jiu, among the crowd, was still unsure whether to believe the story. The boy servant saw Zhao Jiu from afar, called him by his nickname, and cursed him, angry that he did not come immediately to recognize him. When he saw Zhao Jiu's wife, he also cursed her. He pointed out the burn scar on his wife's arm and the place where grain was stored under a tree. From then on, the boy visited both families. The state governor, the royal family member Ronglu, the deputy governor Li Haofu, and the vice-commander Shi Shunyuan were amazed by the event and personally questioned him. The boy said he was first summoned by someone and arrived at a large official residence. A soldier ordered him to wait at the gate. After a long time, the soldier came out and said, "No need to see the master, just follow me." The boy rode a donkey, went several miles, and entered the riverbank. A woman was already there. The soldier pointed to the woman and said, "This is your mother!" In a moment of shock, the boy was pushed into the water by the soldier and lost consciousness. He did not remember anything until he was three years old, when he began to recall his past life.

A Strange Encounter with Liu Zhongyin Liu Zhongyin, from Longshan, was twenty years old when he passed the examination with the inscription "By not valuing rare objects, the people will be content." He then began his official career as a magistrate of Zanhuang. One day, while patrolling, he arrived early at a mountain temple and saw a poem on the wall: "The long branches and layered leaves rustle softly, the cold sound under the pillow lingers for the guest. The wild crane does not come, and the mountain moon sets; the taste of sleeping alone in the fifth watch of autumn." He asked the monk who had written it, and the monk replied, "A guest, about sixty years old, with rich and strange attire, stayed here last night and wrote this poem this morning before leaving. The ink is not yet dry, so he has not gone far." Liu Zhongyin dispatched archers to track the guest down. Soon, the soldiers reported, "The guest is waiting for you under a big tree in the mountains." Liu Zhongyin took wine with him and went to meet the guest, who greeted him with a bow. The guest also reciprocated with a respectful bow. When Liu asked for his name, the guest did not respond but pointed to the wine and asked for a drink. Liu Zhongyin saw that the guest's conversation was graceful and realized he was a strange and extraordinary person. He asked the guest about questions from his life concerning the *Five Classics* and *Transmission*, and the guest answered in detail, revealing things Liu had never heard before. The guest also thought that Liu was someone he could converse with. They raised their cups and drank, inviting their followers to join. As the day grew late, Liu Zhongyin and his attendants all became very drunk. When they woke up, the guest was gone. After this encounter, Liu Zhongyin made great progress in his poetry. His grandson, Li Qinshu, an imperial historian, told me about this.

The Zhuang Clan of Tongshan Forty li north of Lingbi lies a place called Tongshan, where there is a temple known as Nanhuaguan. After Zhuangzi, over two hundred families remain descended from him. The clan leader counts the descendants by their order of birth, and among them are the 28th and 29th elders. The clan leader is officially appointed by the government, given a staff and seal, and is responsible for handling disputes. The local customs are pure and virtuous. Among the people, there are those skilled in discussing the Dao. Jiexiu’s Wu Yuanchang composed a poem titled *Nanhua* and wrote: "Try to grasp the truth from Nanhuang — are life and death not like waking and dreaming? Have waking and dreaming ever ceased through the day and night? Does life and death continue through the ages? Tongshan sees spring grass grow year by year, and Ju water flows with green waves every year. Since Zhuangzi passed away a thousand years ago, how much less time has he spent awake than in dreams?"
A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

The Appearance of a Dragon at the Three Nuns Temple Near the Three Nuns Temple in Daming, a dragon appeared, lying horizontally across three thatched huts. Hundreds of spectators gathered to witness the event. The dragon's scales were a deep yellow, and its shape resembled a camel's hump, with its head level with a large tree. The stench was so foul that it could not be approached. After falling, the dragon could not rise again. After a long time, the clouds and mist reformed, and the dragon departed. This occurred during the seventh or eighth month of the year of Gengyou. Identification of the Mirror Cai Neihan Zhengfu said: In the autumn of the seventh year of Dading, I was serving in the capital with Xiao Yanzhao. One day, Xiao Yanzhao visited me and showed me an ancient mirror, saying: "I recently obtained this from Guanzhong. Although I loved it very much, I did not fully recognize the four characters on the back, and I did not know what period it belonged to." I took a look at it, and it was from the Han dynasty. The inscription reads: "May your descendants thrive." It is recorded in the *Xuanhe Catalogue of Ancient Artifacts*. I showed him the catalogue, and it matched almost perfectly. Xiao Yanzhao was surprised and delighted. Yao Zhongzhan was present, and he said: "In my family, there is a mirror that is also uniquely crafted. It was obtained in the late Song dynasty from a family in Chang'an. It is said to have been a mirror from Yang Guifei's makeup box, but I did not believe it." I asked him to bring it over, and on the back, there were dozens of regular script characters forming a rhythmic poem with four-character lines.

It briefly contains phrases such as "magnificent buildings reflecting each other, pearl curtains facing each other, secretly peering at the sacred and the virtuous, beautiful and consistently upright," and the inscription has the words "Kaiyuan" (the reign title of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang). Yao said, "Considering the year, it is a Tang artifact; how can we know it was once owned by Yang Guifei?" I smiled without answering. Gradually, I took out the *Redundant Record* written by the recluse Zhang Yunshou, and asked him to read it. It records that during the reign of Emperor Shenzong, in the old site of the post station called Gengwang Xianyue, an ancient mirror was found, inscribed with a four-character poem. Among its lines was the phrase "secretly peering at the sacred and the virtuous." The characters "sacred" and "virtuous" were slightly empty, suggesting that "sacred" refers to the sovereign and "virtuous" to the empress. This perfectly matches the inscription. Gengwang Xianyue is several miles from the Ma嵬 Slope, where it was likely left during the imperial journey. Zhang Yunshou, from the right side of Shanxi, obtained it in Chang'an, and indeed, it is true! Yanzhao was greatly delighted, thinking that there were two extraordinary events in one day, and it could not be left unrecorded. I said, "Often speaking and often hitting the mark—this is why Confucius criticized Zi Gong. However, the world enjoys talking about his occasional hits; how can I not write it down?" **Lü Neihan's Last Will** Lü Zhongsi, throughout his life, gained insights from the study of the classics and always set his standards according to ancient sages. On his deathbed, he gave instructions to his sons: "After I die, do not cremate me; cremation is equivalent to mutilating the body. Do not chant by monks or perform Buddhist ceremonies. Such practices are not treating me according to the teachings of Yao, Shun, Wen, Wu, Zhou, and Confucius. If anyone violates my words, they are not a true descendant of the Lü family!" His sons followed his instructions, and none dared to disobey. Fan Sinao and Liang Duyun Dou Nan often spoke to me about this. Recently, Liang Duyun Dou Nan left a will stating, "No Buddhist rituals at the end of life." This practice has been this way from the beginning.

The Mad Monk of Xuan De Temple The mad monk of the Xuan De Temple wore coarse clothes and lived alone in a dark room. In the summer, he did not wash, yet he had no foul odor. He often stood in the temple's stables and clapped his hands, speaking to the cattle and horses, saying, "Enjoy your meal! Enjoy your meal!" The students greatly disliked him. During the reign of Emperor Cheng'an, there was a spring drought. The deputy governor, Mr. Tian, asked, "When will it rain?" The monk replied, "On the 20th day of the fourth month, it will rain enough!" As expected, it rained on the specified day. The governor was brewing wine for the Mid-Autumn Festival, but the monk said, "The governor is going east. Why bother brewing wine?" On the 14th day, the governor was appointed as the magistrate of Xingzhong and rode in the official carriage to take office. Most of what the monk said came true. As told by Fan Zheng. The Imperial Scholar's Dream Lu Zao, the inner historian, styled himself Zicheng. Before passing the imperial examinations, he dreamed of a golden dragon coiling down from the sky, seizing and eating it. That year, he won first place in the provincial examination for Confucian classics and then won first place in the palace examination for poetry and prose. The imperial gate requested a poem from him, which included the lines, "The imperial scholar's family has three generations of imperial scholars, and the family holds two first-place positions in the imperial examinations." This referred to his great-grandfather Yanshi, grandfather Zhongsu, and himself, all of whom were imperial scholars.

**Zhang Ziyun Seeks Immortality** Zhang Ziyun was appointed to an official position through hereditary privilege. He once wrote a *Yuefu* poem titled "Jin Ren Peng Lu Pan" (Golden Man Holding the Dew Tray), describing the joy of retirement, which became widely known at the time. Dao Ling summoned him to serve as the supervisor of painting and calligraphy, and he was gradually promoted to the deputy governor of Jizhou. One day, he sought immortality, and a deity responded with a poem titled "Qing Men Yin," ending with the lines: "A mere piece of paper fame, how many gray hairs does it bring? A single boat plan to return to Wuling, it's better to retire early. I fear the peach blossoms will mock my old age." Zhang Ziyun immediately retired. This story was told by his friend Zhong Shu. **Menggu Asks for a Tree** In the village of Shiluo near Mount Kunlun in Ninghai, there was a wealthy Liu family. They once caught a hundred-zhang fish in the sea, took its bones to make beams, and constructed a grand hall named "Li Tang" (Hall of the Carp). In front of the hall stood a large locust tree, whose shade covered several acres, a rarity in the world. Liu suddenly dreamed of a female official who identified herself as Menggu and asked Liu to give her the locust tree to build a temple. Liu was hesitant in his dream and replied, "The temple is several miles away. How could the tree be moved there?" He then vaguely agreed. Upon waking, he found the dream strange but did not believe it. After several days, a fierce storm arose, and the sky was dark as night. People knew something was happening and hid indoors. Soon after, the storm cleared, and the locust tree was no longer in Liu's yard. People searched for it and found it lying in front of Menggu's temple!

#### Filial Horse During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong, a soldier in the imperial guard owned a dark gray horse that could understand and follow human commands. This soldier had no family members to assist him. Every time he was on duty, the horse would bring its own bedding to the location; when his duty ended, the horse would carry the bedding back home. If someone else tried to obstruct or pull at the horse, it would make threatening noises and bite, and no one dared to approach it. Even when the soldier was away from his unit or elsewhere, the horse would find him, always reaching his location. Once, the soldier fell into a pit during a southern campaign and could not get up; the horse knelt on its front two legs, allowing the soldier to grab the reins and climb onto its back. The story of this horse, known as the "Filial Horse," became widely known. One day, a high-ranking eunuch came to console the troops in the Huai region and playfully ordered the soldier to hide, then released the horse to find him. The horse shook its reins and neighed loudly, and immediately went directly to its owner. The eunuch reported this to Emperor Xuanzong, who then increased the soldier's monthly stipend. #### Scorpion Platform In the northeastern corner of the eastern capital's palace city, there was a platform called the Scorpion Platform. During the reign of Emperor Shizong, when the city walls were being repaired, laborers dismantled the platform to extract soil. When they had dug halfway through, they found a stone box. Upon opening it, they discovered a round, smooth stone that made animal-like sounds when shaken. When the stone was broken, two large scorpions were found with their tails intertwined and rotating, unable to separate. They died as soon as they were exposed to the air. Someone asked Zhang Duyun Fuheng, "There are no scorpions in Liaodong. How did scorpions end up in the stone, in the box, and buried in the earth? How did people know there were scorpions and name the platform after them?" Zhang pondered for a long time and said, "Those who buried the stone box must have known the number. Otherwise, it must have been a divine message." Beyond that, he did not know.

**陵川瑞花** My father served as the magistrate of Lingchuan. On the Lantern Festival of the year Jiazi in the Taihe era, the local school burned lamps, and there were paper flowers cut from colored paper made from dried branches of apricot, pear, and hawthorn trees. After the festival, the household servants asked for them and placed them in the Buddha hall of the county office. On the seventh day of the fourth month, my mother was burning incense and reciting scriptures, and she saw that the apricot and hawthorn branches were all in bloom, mixed with real and fake. My father showed this to his guests, who believed it was an auspicious omen for literature, and he wrote a poem titled *Ruihua Shi* (Poem of Auspicious Flowers). I was just fifteen years old at the time. **不食而孕** In the city of Tokyo, a woman from the family of Niu, who was not yet twenty years old, stopped eating because of a strange dream. If she ate, she would vomit and fall ill. After several years, she did not lose weight. In the year YiSi, she gave birth to a daughter, and she nursed her herself. Her mother-in-law took her to see Master Fan, the Daoist. Master Fan told her mother-in-law, "The woman has not eaten, and she has no bowel movements. Why don't you let her join the Dao?" Her mother-in-law replied, "The woman is attached to her little daughter and cannot leave her." Those who understand think that this woman is eating the vital energy, so she acts normally, and her menstrual cycle has come; but her attachment cannot be severed, so she gave birth. Such cases are not common in books and records.

**Right Armpit Birth** Li Zhanran, the Daoist priest, entered the pass in the autumn of 1238 and personally witnessed a strange birth. A woman who was about to give birth suddenly developed a large wound on her right armpit. When the wound opened, the baby emerged from the wound, and both the mother and child were safe. **Li Moxiang's Prediction** Yang Xiushi, the governor of Wan Prefecture, was appointed as the chief clerk of the Ministry of Punishments during the Zhengda period. He was responsible for supplying goods to Guanzhong and Shaanxi. A fortune-teller named Li Moxiang came from the south and could predict the future with great accuracy. The government considered him a charlatan and kept him under surveillance. Yang became familiar with Li and said to himself, "He can predict the future, but if he says I will live long and have a distinguished career later, it will be comforting. If he says I will not have much time left or will face difficulties in the future, it will only increase my worries!" During a meal, Yang asked Li, "I don't want to know about my fate. But I have been away from my mother for ten months. When will I see her again?" Li, while eating, laughed and said, "You will meet your mother in three days!" Yang thought it was impossible to travel from Guanzhong and Shaanxi, which were over a thousand miles from the capital, in three days. Before he finished his meal, Prime Minister Rui urgently summoned Yang to report on border affairs. Three days later, Yang arrived home before noon. Yang still considers this event miraculous. Li later went to the capital and stayed at Huian Temple. Many court officials visited him to seek predictions. When a court official named Jiao Chunhe entered the temple, Li immediately said, "You will hold a fifth-rank position, but you wish you came from a higher social status!" Jiao was originally a servant of Emperor Shizong of Jin. Upon hearing Li's prediction, he felt ashamed. Li was eventually executed at the age of thirty-three.

### The Virtuous Maiden of the Lei Family The Lei family, from Hunyuan, were related to the Western and Southern branches of the family. At seventeen, she married the deputy magistrate of Yingzhou. Some members of the Lei family who were displeased, accused her of having a pre-marital relationship, and the marriage was then annulled. The deputy magistrate's wife said to her, "Breaking off the marriage was certainly not our intention. But will you remarry?" She replied, "If I remarry, I will make my eyes blind!" The deputy magistrate said, "If you truly have this resolve, I will take the same oath." Later, the deputy magistrate broke his promise and remarried. Not long after, he indeed lost his sight. The Lei maiden remained a widow at eighteen and lived to be ninety-seven years old before passing away. Her grandson, Xiyan, often wanted to write a record of her, but he never got around to it. ### The Punishment for the Green Script In the early years of the Zhenghe era, Liu Shen, a native of Yangqiao in Zhongmu, with the courtesy name Rongfu, wrote a green script to recommend Liu Liu to the gods. Liu had a history of immoral behavior, and Rongfu wrote the script, defending Liu's actions, and followed the usual procedure for recommendation. Several days later, Rongfu became the head. A student named Lu Luo'er, who was suddenly possessed by a spirit while ill, ordered his family to summon Rongfu. When Rongfu arrived, Lu Luo'er scolded him, saying, "You wrote a green script for Liu Liu yesterday. Who gave you the right to speak falsely?" After hearing this, Rongfu was extremely frightened and wrote a confession with his own hands, stating that he would never write another green script again. Lu Luo'er said nothing more. This boy was just a young boy and initially did not know what a green script was. Perhaps Liu's sins were so evident, and Rongfu made false statements to seek merit, so the spirit used this boy to warn him.

People of Lingchuan Pray for Immortals Lingchuan scholar Liu Fangyuanqing said: After the turmoil of war, people in the county prayed for immortals. Yang, the recluse, had a spirit descend and write, and the poetry was particularly extraordinary and remarkable. For instance, when discussing the character "书" (shū), it was said: "Do you know the heart at the tip of the brush, which cannot be obstructed by anything?" It also said: "The dragon coils in one breath, determining the thunder and lightning; the whale transforms into three mountains, causing the grass and trees to wither." One official, during a gathering, presented a sheet of paper from Goryeo and requested a poem, which read: "Frost enters the sharpness of the words, and the moon's trace is incomplete, but in the hand, one does not feel the lightning and thunder." Thus, eight lines were written, followed by a postscript: "Be careful not to touch or stain it! The divine entity will eventually fly away." The wife of the official's son, who had not yet completed her postpartum period, secretly observed the paper. Three days later, when it was opened, there were no words, only an empty sheet of paper. Liu once asked: "What is the nature of life and death?" The spirit replied: "Death, death, hate death, quick death, life is not necessarily enjoyable, death is not necessarily bad. The aversion of ghosts to life is like the aversion of humans to death. But the living do not know that the dead find joy!" County scholar Du Yu, with the courtesy name Shunqing, had always been skeptical of immortals and ghosts.

One day, he summoned Shunqing to the front and said, "I give you a ginkgo." Shunqing asked, "Where is it?" He replied, "It is in your bosom." Shunqing searched his bosom and found it. He then said, "I give you a cherry." Shunqing asked, "How can there be any in the twelfth month?" He replied, "It is already in your hand." Shunqing opened his hand, and indeed there was a cherry. One day, he distributed to many people thirty or more branches of a red apricot tree, which exactly corresponded to the number of people in the assembly. Someone asked, "How can there be such things in the severe winter?" He replied, "This land's winter is not like another place's summer, is it?" Another person asked, "Where is that other place?" He replied, "In a place tens of thousands of miles away." Someone asked, "How can one arrive here in such a short time?" He replied, "Without haste, yet swift; without traveling, yet arrived. Do you not know this?" Yuanqing asked, "Do the immortals truly exist? Please enlighten me!" He replied, "Do you still doubt?" Yuanqing is now a guest in Shuntian and often recounts this story to me. Therefore, I continued to record it with my pen and presented it after the chaos of war and disorder.

Baoyang's Two Green Dragons Forty li northwest of Shuntian is Baoyang Rock, where the Baojiao Temple stands. Within the temple's pond live two green dragons, large and small. The temple is named "Xianji" (Manifest Aid). Ancient steles recount that the "two greens" were first seen during the Sui and Tang Dynasties, bearing the eyes of generals and holding a rank equivalent to that of a prefect. In the fifth year of Chongning, the larger dragon was enfeoffed as the Lord Chonghui, and in the seventh year of Zhenghe, it was promoted to the Duke Lingyi. The smaller dragon was enfeoffed as the Lord Jiapai and later promoted to the Duke Yingze. Each dragon measures about two to three feet in length, with a deep green hue and red and black fine dots between their spines, vaguely forming square victory flowers. When they move, they raise their heads, unlike other snakes. They walk like ropes on trees. They can summon clouds and rain, and their transformations are unpredictable. The locals offer prayers and sacrifices, and when they are given wine, the dragons stand upright, about two feet tall, bending their heads to drink from the cup. Thus, ancient poets wrote, "The green snake stands upright, drinking the wine of the divine." The "two greens" reside in the sunny slopes of the mountains, each with their own kind, numbering in the dozens or even hundreds, and they do not poison or sting. The larger dragon has one blind eye, and its offspring are all the same.

All those in the south of the mountain are mixed snakes, typically venomous. Yet they dare not intrude into the territory of 'Two Greens' on the north side of the mountain. If they do, they are bitten and killed by the 'Two Greens' clan. The two clans also do not go to the south of the mountain. The children in the village are accustomed to them and even hold them with their hands. They often sleep in people's bedding at night, and people do not find it strange. This matter was mentioned by Zhang Yan Gong of the Tang Dynasty, Feng Ying Wang, Xing Zhongliang, and Zhao Shu Zichong of the Song Dynasty, all of whom studied here. There is Xing's Ji Zhi An, the Forgetful Pavilion, and Yan Gong's Stone Cave Study Hall. Pearl Nest. Initially, the mountain rocks collapsed, revealing a hole half the size of a cup or bowl, smooth and shiny without any signs of carving, as if it had a luster. The locals say that a pearl flew out, hence the name. Near the mountain, about three to four miles away, there is Shengxian Village, which belongs to Mancheng and was the former residence of Feng Wang. In the winter of Xin Hai, I and my brothers Mao Zhengqing, Hao Bochang, and Liu Jingzhi visited once. The temple monk Xian, simple and virtuous, was 78 years old at the time and spoke of the beauty of dragons.

Three Auspicious Pavilion Li Duyun, Gao Tangqing, Minister of Revenue, and Zhao Tingyu, Minister of Rites, studied at a temple in a mountain west of Yongping. In the twelfth lunar month, a branch of a peach tree bloomed, a large cicada perched on it, and a bamboo shoot emerged from the bamboo grove. Thus, they named their dwelling "Three Auspicious Pavilion." Later, all three achieved high positions in government, attaining the highest rank. Tomb of Wang Chucun The tomb of Wang Chucun is located northwest of Yanchuan in Quyang, at the Azure Dragon Monument on White Tiger Mountain. In the eighth month of the year 1579, people from Wan Prefecture raided and destroyed the tomb. The bones were reduced to ashes, and they found over a hundred silver coins, a writing stone, a mirror, and an imperial decree from Emperor Ai of Tang. The decree, inscribed with golden characters, read: "Order to bury the loyal minister Wang Chucun and grant him nine million nine hundred ninety-nine thousand nine hundred ninety-nine coins and nine hundred ninety-nine wen." This was recounted by his grandson, Zhou Chen.

A Girl of Early Wisdom Zhang Wanheng of Shuntian, an official of high rank, had an eighth daughter named Du'e. She was endowed with exceptional grace and intelligence, her eyes so expressive that they seemed to reach her eyebrows. In the autumn of Bingwu (1366 AD), she entered primary school. By the age of seven, she could recite hundreds of words daily. By the second month of Wu Shen (1368 AD), she was composing poetry. She had memorized the *Classic of Filial Piety*, the *Analects*, the *Mencius*, parts of the *Yijing* (Book of Changes) from the *Qian* hexagram to the *Xiaxi* section, the *Shijing* (Book of Poetry) *Ernan* collection, the *Quli*, *Neize*, *Shaoyi*, *Zhongyong*, *Daxue*, *Ruxing*, *Jisong*, *Jiyi*, *Jingjie*, and *Guangun* chapters, as well as Ban Zhao's *Ladies' Admonitions*, Hao's *Neize*, *Nixun*, and *Tongsangji* (six volumes). She recited two poems daily, both ancient and regulated, up to ten in total. In calligraphy, her writing already showed the style of an adult. At home, she would rebuke family members if she saw them behaving improperly or not adhering to proper etiquette. She also understood the main ideas of the books she read and sometimes explained them. Her talent in composing poetry was quick and agile, and she wrote without the usual speech of a child: "Sleepy thoughts are like those of someone drunk, her quiet heart is like that of a monk. Peach and plum blossoms in the east wind, dreams of butterflies; the moon over mountains and passes, the soul of the cuckoo." Scholars considered this poem to be poor. Later, she indeed fell ill and died four days later. She was just nine years old. Hao Bochang wrote a poem to mourn her.

The Shedding-Shell Paulownia In the Shouning Temple of Dai Prefecture, during the Tian Sheng period of the Song Dynasty, an old and withered paulownia tree stood. A legendary alchemist, known as Hai Chanzi, passed through the town selling the elixir of immortality. For three days, he was unable to sell his wares. He then threw the elixir into the tree. The following year, the withered branches sprouted anew, and people called it the "Shedding-Shell Paulownia." Bai Hao, a poet from the Jin Dynasty, wrote a poem about it, which reads: "A single grain of cinnabar wondrously possesses divine power, capable of reviving a withered tree and bringing back spring. The immortal's intention is truly difficult to comprehend, for he only transforms the withered paulownia, not the people." During the Taihe period of the Jin Dynasty, Wang Jiayan passed by Shouning and playfully remarked to Bai Hao, "Your poem criticizes the people of the temple. Do you still have the stone inscription engraved?" Bai then covered the stone inscription. The Golden Treasure Plate During the Xuanzheng period, a Daoist adept could transform mud into gold, creating an object known as the "Golden Treasure Plate." It was three and a half inches long and two and a half inches wide, inscribed with the words: "Forever to protect the blessed land." Two such plates were found in the Tianqing and Shouning temples of Dai Prefecture. The one from Tianqing is still in existence today. During the peaceful times, people circulated and admired it, and it was evident that it was made of mud, with the inscriptions clearly visible.

Tooth-Cleaning Formula** The formula consists of: Poria, gypsum, dragon bone, each one liang; cold water stone, two liang and a half; angelica root, half a liang; fine cinnamon, five qian; large stone sturgeon, one piece; small stone sturgeon, a pair. Grind these into powder and use them to brush your teeth morning and evening. Fanzhou Wang Wen Hanqing obtained this formula from Lin Fu Zhe Shou, who in turn obtained it from the early period military leader Li Cheng of Luoyang. Zhe was over ninety years old, and his teeth were neither loose nor affected by wind or parasites. Wang Wen is also now ninety years old and can still chew meat with his teeth. This truly demonstrates the miraculous efficacy of the formula. **Stone Tablet Fish** In the sea, there is a fish whose tail and feet are no different from those of a turtle. On its back, it carries a shell that stands upright like a stone tablet. When the tide recedes, the fish come ashore to expose their shells, gathering in groups of ten or a hundred. Upon hearing human voices, they dig into the sand and return to the sea. People living by the sea call them "Stone Tablet Fish." Whether they are fish or a kind of beast, it is uncertain. According to ancient lore, the Pu Lao sea creature roars when a whale jumps, and its sound is like a bell. Today, people cast bells in the shape of Pu Lao and engrave the hammer that strikes the bell as a whale, drawing inspiration from both. It seems that ancient people made objects to resemble things, such as boats, chariots, bows, arrows, mortars, and pestles, not without reason. Perhaps the design of stone tablets also has its origins? Or did people give it this name because the fish resembles a stone tablet? **Divine Message to Xuxin Gong** Xuxin Gong once dreamed that the god of Mount Tai told him, "Respect me and you will not gain merit; disrespect me and you will not suffer harm. Follow the path of virtue, and your family will prosper for a long time." He often shared this message with others. This event is recorded in family records.

Frogs Turning into Rats In the Baiyangdian region of Anzhou, in the southern part of Yan, which spans forty li north to south and seventy li east to west, it was once entirely covered by water. Recently, in the year of the Horse (1904), it suddenly dried up. All the frogs and toads in the pond transformed into black rats, which devoured all the roots of the reeds. The soil became loose and soft, requiring no plowing or cultivation; simply planting wheat seeds resulted in a successful harvest. The local residents could not manage the abundance, allowing tenants to harvest and only collecting a small tax. This area, with its dense and firmly rooted grass, was not suitable for farming, yet the transformation of rats into wheat was also an extraordinary event. There is an inscription on a stone that reads: "In times of chaos and disorder, do not leave this pond; if there is water, eat fish; if there is no water, eat noodles." Is it possible that wheat was also obtained before? According to Zhang Hou Deming. A Strange Object Found in a Donkey's Stomach In the old Yongping County of Wanzhou, a family that ground grain owned a donkey. Suddenly, the donkey fell ill. When it died, it cried out for seven days and nights without stopping. Upon dissection, a strange object was found in its large abdomen. It was neither iron nor stone, shaped like a gourd but narrower, with a deep brown color and as hard as iron or stone. The family did not find it unusual and threw it into the wheat storage. Daily rations of wheat were taken from there, and there was no reduction in the supply. This continued for a year, and the villagers began to believe it was a divine object. The official Shi Sheng took it away and placed it in the wheat storage, but there was no miraculous change. It is now in the home of Zhang Hou in Shuntian, and I have seen it myself. A Sheep's Head Falling from the Sky In the second year of the Zhenyou era (1214), Yang Yunqing of Shuzhou served as the magistrate of Gou County. During a summer storm, a sheep's head fell from the sky, about the size of a carriage wheel, with horns standing upright and three feet high. The magistrate reported this strange occurrence to the state of Dai. The military supply warehouse collected it, and the news reached the imperial court.

Disasters in Guanzhong during the Year Dinghai In the fourth year of the Zhengda era (丁亥), two disasters occurred in Guanzhong: In the western pasture of Pingliang, the statue of the Heavenly King swayed back and forth for two days and nights without stopping, yet the clay clothes did not peel off. The governor, Tu Dan Baiqian, went to pay respects to it. After bowing three times, the statue stopped moving. When the governor left, it resumed its motion as before. In Lin洮, mice made noise day and night, and every house was affected. One night near the fourth watch, a group of mice emerged, with one large white mouse leading the way, guiding the others out through the south gate. The archers lying in wait at the gate were trampled on as the mice rushed past. Some mice who did not manage to escape entered the southeast White Grass Plain and were no longer seen. The distance from the city to the White Grass Plain is sixty li. This account was provided by Liu Shanfu's younger brother, Runzhi. Pearl Ice Outside the walls of Lin洮, the洮 River formed small ice particles in winter, resembling water chestnuts, round and clean like pearls. Wealthy residents of the city collected and stored these ice particles. In the height of summer, they mixed them with honey syrup, creating a powder as smooth as real pearls. Along the river for three hundred li, the ice appeared to stretch endlessly when viewed from afar in winter, but if one stepped on it, it would sink. The ice pearls, though extremely cold, did not melt into a single mass. Jade Butterfly Flower in Carbon Valley Ten li southwest of Hu County is called "Carbon Valley." Five li into the valley, there is a tree of jade flowers. The tree is so large that four people must hold hands to encircle it. It blooms only during leap years. It begins to flower at the beginning of the first ten-day period of summer and continues until the end of the third ten-day period. The flowers are white as jade, clustering together in a shape resembling a gathering of eight immortals. Among the flowers, there is one jade butterfly that rises above them. When the flowers fall, they do not touch the ground but rise into the air. After the chaos, the tree was cut down by soldiers.

Ancient Coins A man from Dongping named Qian Xinzong, following the *Qianpu* (Catalog of Coins), collected ancient coins. He gathered dozens of varieties and gave them to Liu Liu, who ran a tea shop. Liu was from a family associated with the *Cao Si* (a government office responsible for grain transportation) and was known for his interest in collecting. As he collected more ancient coins, he combined them with those from Qian Xinzong's collection, storing them in brocade pouches. If the *Qianpu* did not record the era of a coin, it was placed at the front of the collection; Liu also had some of these. The *jin cuo dao* (coins with gold inlay) were particularly heavy and thick, and today only a few are seen. There were also *fang cun bi* (small coins), which had the same design as the *cuo dao* and were the most numerous, considered the finest among all the coins. The large coins of Wang Mang, shaped like swallow tails, were four times the size of modern coins. They bore the inscription "Duan Bu Dang Qian" (meaning "Duan Bu is worth a thousand"). On the reverse side, there were two characters: "Si Bu," "Quan Bu," "Huo Bu," and "Liu Bu," among others, nearly ten in total. There were also coins weighing one *zhu*, two *zhu*, three *zhu*, up to five *zhu*. Some had *nei si chu wen* (a pattern on the inner edge), featuring square holes, four corners, and gold inscriptions. Others had *qian bei si chu wen* (a pattern on the back), resembling *yu jia* (a type of coin), one called "Wu Jin" and another "Wu Zhu." It seems the character "zhu" was divided into two. There were both "yong jin" (coins with gold waves) and "lou jin" (coins with gold carvings). The *Kaiyuan Qian* (coins from the Kaiyuan period of the Tang Dynasty) had *yong jin yue ya* (gold wave crescents) and *lou jin yue ya* (gold carving crescents), with some having a horizontal *yong jin yue ya* line running through the rim, and others having a vertical *yong jin yue ya* line running through the rim. This collection was not limited to ancient coins; it also included books, paintings, musical instruments, and other antiquities. Scholars and officials gathered there daily, while ordinary guests were excluded. After the fall of Dongping, it is unknown whether the ancient artifacts still exist.

The Spirit Saves General Zhen's Army In the year of the Rabbit, the commander of Dingzhou, Zhen Quan, was attacked by the northern army. He sought help from the Hengshan army, but they delayed their advance. Zhen Quan jumped over the city wall to escape and ultimately died, being captured by the northern army. The Hengshan army regarded Zhen Quan as a traitor and requested the execution of Zhen's clan members who were in the army. They also kidnapped Zhen Quan and took him to the Toushan stronghold. Half of the people in the stronghold went out to transport grain, and the external army suddenly arrived. The defenders did not prepare for it, and many enemies were killed. Those transporting grain did not know they were being kidnapped and returned to the stronghold calmly. The Hengshan army secretly watched them, planning to kill all and then stop. That night, a large green ghost appeared on the stronghold, with eyes like cups, red, and with flames. The soldiers were frightened and scattered and ran away, and the Zhen group was able to escape.

A Pig of Virtue In the west of Luoyang, in the village of Yongning, there was a butcher's shop where dozens of pigs were raised. One day, a young apprentice asked the head butcher which pig should be slaughtered. The butcher climbed into the pen to point out a pig, causing the other pigs to become startled and restless, except for one pig that remained calm and still. The butcher pointed to this pig and said, "This pig eats very little and has been raised for a long time; it can be slaughtered." The apprentice entered the pen and dragged the pig, tying it up.
